news.title: Decoding Charli XCX's new album, Music, Fashion, Film
news.text:
Decoding Charli XCX's new album, Music, Fashion, Film

Charli XCX was supposed to be taking a break.

Her sixth album, Brat, was the sort of record you could genuinely call era-defining, without being accused of overstating its significance. 

Experimental but accessible songs like Guess, Apple and 360 propelled the star to new heights of fame. 

Her album was co-opted into Kamala Harris's 2024 presidential campaign, and Collins English Dictionary updated its definition of Brat, to acknowledge its use as an adjective characterising the music's "confident, independent, and hedonistic attitude". 

After a punishing year-long tour, the singer accepted a few acting roles, wrote the soundtrack to Emerald Fennel's adaptation of Wuthering Heights and married her longterm partner George Daniel.

But instead of settling down, she found herself back in the studio.

"Out of the blue, inspiration struck me," she says. "I suddenly felt like I had something to say, so I ran with it."

In a matter of weeks, she dashed off 11 short, guitar-based songs that tried to make sense of her whirlwind two years.

The resulting album, titled Music, Fashion, Film, is smart, self-referential, funny, antagonistic and crammed full of moments to stimulate your inner earworm.

Significantly, deliberately, it's not Brat 2.0. 

"If I'd made another album that felt more dance-leaning, it would have felt really hard, really sad," she told Vogue in the first interview for the album.

"To be boring is to die right there on the spot," she added in a newsletter to fans. "Give up, give in, go home, stay home, end it all."

As a result, there's a lot to uncover in the music and lyrics. Here's what we learned.

It's not a rock album

In the aforementioned Vogue article, Charli played the interviewer a track called Rock Music, featuring the lyric: "I think the dancefloor is dead, so now we're making rock music".

The publication took that as gospel, calling Music, Fashion, Film the star's "rock reinvention". 

Fans recoiled. Courtney Love accused Charli of being a troll. Madonna captioned a photo, "If your dance floor feels dead, maybe you're playing the wrong music."

But while guitars are the album's predominant sound, it's far from being a rejection of club music. Even the song Rock Music is full of manipulated vocals and electronic flourishes.

"I never said I was making a rock album," Charli posted on Instagram, explaining that the song was really about her relationship with the Brat album. 

"I've made electronic-leaning music for over a decade," she told music YouTuber Nicky Reardon. 

"That's shaped who I am as an artist. [Rock Music] is kind of about my pivot from that as an artist who doesn't want to continue to do the same thing over and over and over again."

Three icons appear on the cover

Rather than a picture of Charli, the album is being promoted with a stylish black-and-white photo of Velvet Underground musician John Cale, fashion designer Marc Jacobs, and film director Martin Scorsese.

The trio epitomise excellence in their respective fields - reflecting the album's overarching theme of art, excellence and immortality.

The photo was taken in New York this April, by Scottish filmmaker Aidan Zamiri, who's been behind the camera for some of Charli's recent music videos, as well as her mockumentary The Moment.

"Honestly it was a really emotional day," the singer told BBC 6 Music's Nick Grimshaw. 

"I was like, 'Wow, these three people mean so much to me personally as an artist'. And I couldn't believe that they were all together, you know? 

"We were so nervous and then when it was happening it was just honestly really sweet."

Charli addresses the physical toll of touring Brat

On Rock Music, Charli admits that the head-banging physicality of the Brat tour ended up giving her nerve damage in her neck.

"It really impacts a lot on my sleep habits," she told Sam Murphy on the Pop Pod, saying the pain had got "progressively worse over the past couple of years". 

"When I think about touring this record I do think I should probably be taking a different approach," she added. "But who knows where I'll end up on that front, you know? Sometimes I just can't stop myself."

Fans got a glimpse of how things might change at a secret show in London last week, where Charli performed some of her new material for the first time.

Flanked by a tight, three piece band, the set was less demanding from a body-slamming perspective, without sacrificing the energy of the music.

Reviews weren't allowed - but let's just say the new material, with its distorted guitars and punchy drums, sounded even better live.

Charli is still trying to work out who she is

Charli's new songs find her grappling with whether she can stay true to her pugnacious, subversive roots while enjoying commercial success and personal happiness.

The slinky single SS26 discusses the pressure on pop stars to do and say the right things. 

Lyrically, Charli disavows cigarettes and writes sincere apologies for her past mistakes. She even talks about using her politic as a "press strategy", noting that her part-Indian heritage "could give me quite the USP".

Laced with sarcasm, it highlights the idiocy of holding celebrities to unattainable standards, instead of letting music (and fashion and film) explore the darker side of human nature. 

"If you're on the internet, there's this feeling like you need to be on the right side of every single conversation," Charli told Nicky Reardon. 

"You can't just be good. You have to be the right type of good, in a specific way and if not, you're a bad person who actually had a sinister motive this entire time. So the song is actually talking about my experience with that."

Equally compelling is the single Wink Wink, where Charli emphasises her newfound maturity - wearing trousers, driving sensible cars - before admitting it's all a pose.

"I'm not a bad girl anymore / Wink, wink."

But the most nakedly honest lyric comes on I'm Afraid - potentially the least romantic song ever written about getting engaged.

"Happiness came in a box," Charli says of the proposal, before spiralling into a panic about who she'll become if she says "yes".

"I'm a wife, I'm a kid / I'm destroyed, I'm a bitch / I'm too drunk, I can't sleep / I can't feel, I'm afraid / I'm nothing without him / What if I ruin it?" she sings, as discordant synths rip apart the sonic fabric.

Connecting the songs is the idea that everyone's flawed - but the mess and the friction make life more interesting.

The minimalism is intentional

If the lyrics on Brat were direct and unembellished, Charli's new album takes minimalism to an extreme.

The seventh song, Yeah, is a textbook example of tension and release in pop music - that strips the idea down to its most elemental form. 

For the first 30 seconds, Charli simply repeats the phrase, "I don't wanna," over and over while a guitar chugs in the background.

Then, just when you're about to hit the off switch, she declares: "Wait! Now I wanna feel it," and the music explodes into life.

It's so stupid and basic, and yet it scratches a tried-and-tested musical itch with such precision that you have to admire its audacity.

Yeah is the most demo-like song on a 29-minute album that occasionally feels under-cooked. But the brevity was quite deliberate.

"I wasn't like, 'Oh, I must make an album with tiny songs'," Charli told Nick Grimshaw. 

"That was just the way that the songs came out. It was just a choice that felt right for the music that we were making."

David Cronenberg closes the album

Charli is an avowed film buff, whose fifth album was named after David Cronenberg's notorious body-horror film Crash.

The Canadian director is also the last voice you hear on Music, Fashion, Film, in an excerpt of an "hour-long" conversation he had with Charli last year,.

"What's crazy is he actually spoke in tune with the song, without ever having heard it," she told Australia's Triple J radio station.

In the clip, Cronenberg talks about the legacy of art, and whether longevity really matters. 

"Yes, some artists have created art that has really survived for thousands of years, and that's fantastic," he says. "But experientially, for them, they are dead. They are gone. They're not experiencing that."

That's not a cause for sadness, he insists. It means you shouldn't be burdened by the success (or failure) of your work.

"Oblivion is freedom."

It feels like he's showing Charli the escape route from post-Brat expectations. On the evidence of this album, she's already made it through the door.
news.author: Mark Savage

news.title: Ted Cruz Makes 2028 Predictions About AOC and Zohran Mamdani
news.text: Texas Republican Senator Ted Cruz is looking well beyond the current political cycle, predicting major shake-ups within the Democratic Party ahead of the 2028 election. In a video post on X Thursday, Cruz said he believes Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, widely known as AOC, will launch a presidential campaign, while New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani will replace longtime Senator Chuck Schumer. Cruz framed the predictions as "bad for America," saying that some Republicans are cheering opponents who are "crazy." He later added that "this country needs two parties that actually love America." In a separate post, Cruz said, "Chuck Schumer is finished. He can never again win a Democrat primary in New York. He knows it. Which is why I joke that he will be the inaugural member of the AOC for President Campaign." Newsweek reached out to Mamdani, Schumer and Ocasio-Cortez's offices via email for additional comment. What To Know Cruz's comments come as speculation over the 2028 presidential race continues to grow, despite the election still being years away. While no major Democratic candidate has officially declared a campaign, Ocasio-Cortez has been asked about a potential White House run, fueling discussions about her political ambitions. Her national profile, large social media following, and influence among progressive voters have made her one of the most closely watched figures in the party. Mamdani, meanwhile, has become an increasingly prominent progressive voice in New York politics, leading to growing speculation about his long-term political future. Recent Democratic primaries have also highlighted the growing influence of the party's progressive wing, with left-leaning candidates scoring several victories over more established figures. Those wins have fueled speculation that progressive leaders could play an even larger role in shaping the Democratic Party's direction heading into the 2028 election cycle. The conversation also comes as new polling highlights an increasingly fluid picture for the 2028 race. On the Republican side, recent surveys indicate Vice President JD Vance remains a leading contender, though Secretary of State Marco Rubio has gained significant ground in recent polling, underscoring how quickly the political landscape could change as both parties look ahead. No candidate has formally announced their candidacy. What Polls Show Wednesday's University of New Hampshire Survey Center poll shows that Ocasio-Cortez has 22 percent compared to former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg's 21 percent. Arizona Senator Mark Kelly landed 9 percent, California Governor Gavin Newsom has 8 percent, Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders has 6 percent and former Vice President Kamala Harris has 5 percent. No other candidate obtained 5 percent or more in the poll. The poll surveyed 648 likely 2028 Democratic presidential primary voters, with a margin of error of 3.8 percent, and 579 likely 2028 Republican presidential primary voters, with a margin of error of 4.1 percent, from July 15 to July 20. When asked about leading in the poll on Wednesday, Ocasio-Cortez told The Independent, "I think there's two things, one is that, you know, polls are polls, and a horse race is a horse race. I am, of course, I think, very deeply humbled and grateful to, you know, folks who participate and convey that confidence." "I think my main focus is really genuinely just the midterms right now, and we have to make sure that we win this majority," Ocasio-Cortez later added. An Emerson survey released Thursday shows Buttigieg leading the Democratic primary field with 19 percent, while other prominent Democrats remained within striking distance. Newsom received 17 percent, both Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff and Congresswoman Ocasio-Cortez landed 13 percent, Harris has 8 percent, Kentucky Governor Andy Beshear has 7 percent, Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro has 5 percent, and Illinois Governor JB Pritzker landed 3 percent. The poll shows that 13 percent are undecided. “Ossoff was added to the Democratic ballot test this month, and entered at 13%, driven by stronger support among men, at 18%, compared to 8% among women,” Spencer Kimball, executive director of Emerson College Polling, said in the survey. “Newsom is strongest among women at 25%, compared to 8% of men. Similarly, Rep. Ocasio-Cortez’s support is higher among women, at 16%, compared to 9% of men. Buttigieg, on the other hand, has equal support among both men and women at 19%.” The poll was conducted from July 19 to July 20 among 1,100 likely voters, and has a credibility interval of 2.9 percent.
news.summary: The conversation comes as new polling highlights an increasingly fluid picture for the 2028 race.
news.author: Anna Commander

news.title: Trump's Base Is Turning Against Him on the Economy: Poll
news.text: A growing number of Republicans are blaming President Donald Trump’s policies for making the economy worse, according to a poll from the Pew Research Center released on Thursday. Trump campaigned on bringing prices down as the country grappled with a high inflation rate in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, and his focus on cost-of-living issues helped carry him to victory in key swing states against former Vice President Kamala Harris in the 2024 election. But 1 1/2 years into his second term—and as the midterm elections approach—most Americans are still unhappy with the state of the economy, recent polling shows. Economists point to policies like tariffs and the Iran war as putting pressure on the economy. Notably, the poll comes as gas prices have begun to rise once again amid renewed fighting in the Middle East. Disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical passageway for oil, caused prices to soar since the United States and Israel launched strikes against Iran in February. Gas prices were $4.091 nationally on Thursday, according to AAA. The new poll found that more Republican voters say Trump’s policies have had a worsening effect on the economy, though most of the president's conservative voter base still supports him. Democrats have sought to make economic concerns central to their campaigns ahead of the midterms, as polls show that many voters point to the economy as their top issue for November. White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers previously told Newsweek that Trump “remains committed to unleashing American energy dominance, cutting costs, and putting more money back in the pockets of hardworking American families.” Newsweek reached out to the White House for comment via email. Republicans Grow More Wary of Economy, But Most Still Approve of Trump The poll found that 28 percent of Republican voters said they believe Trump’s economic policies have made economic conditions worse in the United States. That’s up from 18 percent in January 2026 and September 2025, according to Pew. Overall, 60 percent of respondents blamed Trump’s policies for making the economy worse in the poll. Just 24 percent of all voters—including 10 percent of Democrats and 41 percent of Republicans—described the economy as “excellent” or "good” in the poll. The last time a majority of respondents told Pew they believed the economy was in good condition was January 2020, before the COVID-19 pandemic prompted lockdowns and a global economic slowdown. Meanwhile, 19 percent of Republicans said they believe the economy will be worse in one year, an increase from 14 percent in January 2026, when that number was 14 percent, but the same amount as in September 2025. Despite these economic concerns, most Republicans remain firmly supportive of Trump—69 percent said they approve of his job performance, compared to 29 percent who disapproved of him, according to the poll. Among those who identified as a conservative Republican, his net approval rating stood at +54 points. Economy Is Major Midterm Issue Other polls show the economy will be a major issue that voters are thinking about when they head to the polls in November. A poll from The Economist and YouGov found that 32 percent of Americans named inflation and prices as their top issue, while an additional 14 percent said jobs and the economy were their top issue and 10 percent said it was healthcare. In that poll, only 21 percent said the economy was excellent or good, compared to 75 percent who said it was in fair or poor condition. The poll surveyed 1,602 adults from July 17-20 and had a margin of error of plus or minus 3.3 percentage points. Democrats have emphasized the economy in their messaging ahead of this November’s elections. “As I’ve spoken with North Carolinians across the state, folks have been open about their struggles. Rising grocery prices, surprise billing from a doctor’s visit, expensive child care. These struggles and the people affected by them are why I’m running for the US Senate,” Roy Cooper, the former Democratic governor of North Carolina who is now running for Senate, wrote in a Wednesday post on X. The White House has sought to highlight economic achievements. “This was the biggest tax refund season in American history, with the average refund exceeding $3,400, an 11 percent increase over the past year. American families and workers claimed $82 billion in direct tax relief, and 97 percent of filers received a tax cut,” White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said during a briefing on Thursday. A Fox News poll found that Democrats hold their strongest lead over Republicans since 2006 among registered voters regarding their trust in handling the economy. When asked "Which political party -- the Democrats or the Republicans -- do you think would do a better job" on the economy, Democrats landed 54 percent compared to 45 percent saying Republicans, marking a 9-point net advantage for Democrats. In April, Democrats had 52 percent compared to Republicans' 48 percent. The last time Democrats had a net positive of 9 points or more was 20 years ago, in a July 2006 poll, the survey shows. Trump Unveils New Tariffs New tariffs have gone into effect this week, including 50 percent tariffs on goods imported from Canada and 25 percent tariffs on goods imported from Canada. Tariffs have become a cornerstone of Trump’s economic policy, as the president touts them as necessary to restore domestic manufacturing jobs and close the trade deficit with other countries. Critics, however, have lambasted the tariffs as a policy that would result in higher prices for American consumers. On Thursday, Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced that the administration would impose new tariffs on 60 countries “for their failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor.” “President Trump recognizes that decades of moral suasion have not eradicated forced labor from global supply chains. The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it’s well past time for our trading partners to do the same,” Greer wrote in a statement. Trump Approval Rating: Latest Polls Recent polling shows that Trump’s approval has fallen since returning to the White House. Here is a look at some of them: PollsterApproveDisapproveFox News (July 17-20)39%61%YouGov (July 17-20)36%60%Emerson College (July 19-20)39%57%Verasight (July 14-17)38%59%Ipsos/Washington Post (July 8-13)37%61%
news.summary: A growing number of Republicans blame Trump's policies for making the economy worse, according to a Pew Research Center poll.
news.author: Andrew Stanton

news.title: Watch: Rapper Lil Wayne Comes Out in Full Support of Trump's White House Ballroom
news.text:
Rap legend Lil Wayne praised President Donald Trump for constructing a ballroom at the White House and defending his decision to build it, adding that an indoor venue for performers and other major events is necessary, rather than having to host them “on the damn lawn.”

“I’m so glad to hear Trump say — when they was mad at him about building the ballroom — and he was, like, ‘Because everything we do, we have to do it on the damn lawn,'” Lil Wayne said during a Monday appearance on the Friends Keep Secrets podcast.

“People who don’t know nothing [are] hearing that thinking he’s just being Trump… no, for real, I was glad to hear him say that,” the “Lollipop” rapper continued.

Lil Wayne then recalled a past experience he had during an outdoor performance for then-Vice President Kamala Harris’ event marking the 50th anniversary of hip-hop in 2023.

The hip-hop artist compared the event — which was hosted at the vice-presidential residence, Number One Observatory Circle — to a “backyard barbeque,” and remarked that he was surprised to learn he wasn’t even invited inside.

“I had to perform probably, like, two years ago,” Lil Wayne explained. “When I got there and had to perform, like, why is it in the back? I was like, ‘What?’ I was like, ‘I can’t go in?’ So this is like a backyard barbecue?”

“That’s literally where they do everything at,” the “I’m the One” singer lamented of outdoor White House-related performances.

As Breitbart News previously reported, Lil Wayne came out in support of the President Trump during the 2020 election, revealing he met with the president and discussed criminal justice reform and Trump’s plan to invest in black communities and businesses.

“Just had a great meeting with @realdonaldtrump @potus besides what he’s done so far with criminal reform, the platinum plan is going to give the community real ownership,” Lil Wayne said at the time, sharing a photo of himself standing beside President Trump.

“He listened to what we had to say today and assured he will and can get it done,” the acclaimed hip-hop artist added.

As for the ballroom, President Trump has commented that the new venue will host state dinners, performances, and other major White House-related events.

Notably, the ballroom would also be going up after this year’s White House Correspondents Dinner offsite, which suffered a shooting that put the president and various Cabinet officials, among others, in danger.
news.summary:
Rap legend Lil Wayne praised President Donald Trump for constructing a ballroom at the White House and defending his decision to build it, adding that an indoor venue for performers and other major events is necessary, rather than having to host them "on the damn lawn."


The post Watch: Rapper Lil Wayne Comes Out in Full Support of Trump’s White House Ballroom appeared first on Breitbart.
news.author: Alana Mastrangelo

news.title: Sad Girl Summer Is Upon Us: Olivia Rodrigo, Gracie Abrams, and Phoebe Bridgers All Have the Blues
news.text:
First came Rodrigo, whose batch of budding love songs morphed into a thorny breakup record. On her third studio album, You Seem Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love, released in mid-June, she grappled with the weeds of anxiety that grew in her blossoming romance with British actor Louis Partridge, from whom she split last December. Referring to herself as “unraveled,” and a “sad shell of a woman” at various points on the record, Rodrigo ultimately comes to the conclusion that her malaise has very little to do with a man, singing on the song “The Cure:” “It don’t matter how your love feels anymore / It’ll never be the cure.”
Sharing personal demons and political passions go hand in hand for 23-year-old Rodrigo. “I am an artist, and what I do for my job is ‘this is how I feel,’ and present it to people,” she recently told The New York Times. “And I think it would just be disingenuous to be like, I don’t feel heartbroken about what’s going on in Gaza. You know what I mean?” In August, Rodrigo will host and headline her own inaugural one-day music festival called Daisy Chain Fields, a spiritual successor to Lilith Fair with an all-female lineup including Stevie Nicks, Doechii, and Mitski. All net proceeds will go toward nonprofits advocating for women and girls.
That’s also been true of Abrams, 26, who campaigned for Kamala Harris in the last presidential election and now confesses to “a new spiral every night,” on her latest album Daughter from Hell, released in mid-July. On the song “Humming,” Abrams sings about the fires that in January last year ravaged the Pacific Palisades neighborhood where she was raised. “Every kid I grew up with has lost their childhood house / No way to make sense of it, it’s why I’m making sound,” she sings. But Abrams is concerned with more than her own backyard. “There’s no one at the top to believe / What a way to feel in your twenties,” she sings of a youthful disillusionment no doubt felt by some of her pop contemporaries. “Do we stay numb?” Abrams wonders. “But you can’t when you’re seeing / How they all get off on our grieving.”
Elsewhere on the record, insecurity bleeds into her love story with Irish actor Paul Mescal: “If you could do me just a little favor, tell me I’m no failure?” Abrams sings in “Imaginary Friend,” the song on which Mescal is a credited cowriter). One might assume some thematic connective tissue to new music from Mescal’s ex Phoebe Bridgers, whose next album Lost Weekend, slated for release August 14, will be her first solo effort in six years. On the lead single “Lost Boys,” Bridgers sings about her lack of regret, but not remorse, in beginning a new chapter: “You told me you wish you were dead, but I don’t believe that,” she croons. “I still wonder how you’re sleeping / And I don’t feel bad, but I’m sorry.”
Another singer-songwriter in indie pop known for ballads about heartbreak, including 2024’s mournful track “Spring Into Summer,” is Lizzy McAlpine, who this week announced her next album Angel will be released on September 18. Although these last two records have yet to be released, some social media users have drawn presumed thematic lines between these artists’ summer musical output, coining the name “thought daughters” for themselves and their own states of unwellness.
Even Charli xcx and Ariana Grande, previously optimistic purveyors of pop, are hitting a wall. Grande, most recently known for her uplifting belts as Glinda the Good Witch in Wicked, is feeling undeniably downbeat on her latest single “Hate That I Made You Love Me,” delivering the dour message that “it’s all bad news” at the moment. There’s also a nihilistic tilt to singles off Charli’s new album Music, Fashion, Film. She may have wanted to “dance all night” back when Kamala was Brat, but the present moment has made this Brit go bleak. In “Rock Music,” she declares “the dance floor is dead.” And on “SS26”—short for “spring-summer 2026”—she sings: “The world is gonna end, no hope for any of it / Yeah, we’re walkin’ on a runway that goes straight to hell / Nothing’s gonna save us, not music, fashion, or film.”
It’s impossible to consider the current crop of female pop stars without mention of Taylor Swift (who might not be having a sad girl summer considering she married Travis Kelce in a star-studded ceremony at Madison Square Garden earlier this month)—half of the previously mentioned artists have opened for her on tour. This new music no doubt took cues from the pandemic-summer hit album Folklore. But it’s also worth noting that all of these artists were adolescents when “Summertime Sadness,” Lana Del Rey’s enduring portrait of seasonal melancholia, released as the second single off her breakthrough album Born to Die (2012) following “Video Games”—a Rodrigo favorite. Billie Eilish in particular has cited Del Rey’s music as foundational, saying Born to Die, released when Eilish was just 10 years old, “changed music,” in a 2023 podcast interview with Dua Lipa. “And especially changed music for girls and the potential of what is possible.” Or, as Eilish put it when introducing Del Rey for a surprise onstage Coachella duet the following year: “[She] is the reason for half you bitches existence, including mine.”
Like those who came before, the current class of pop stars are meeting the moment with music that reflects their own uncertainties. Between impending war, threats of AI, and what feels like daily outbreaks of foodborne illness—who can blame Gracie, Olivia, and the like for rising to the occasion of an undeniably cruel summer.
news.summary: And they aren’t the only ones having a cruel, cruel summer. Amid anxieties both personal and geopolitical, even pop stars like Ariana Grande and Charli xcx are singing about our uncertain reality.
news.author: Savannah Walsh

news.title: Historian exposes dark Civil War secret behind Trump’s master plan
news.text:
Even though Americans are largely rejecting his election denial conspiracy theories, President Donald Trump does not care because he is not only hoping to steal the 2026 midterms — he plans on turning America into the Confederacy, right down to the white supremacy and lack of democracy, at least according to a prominent historian.

Thom Hartmann, author of books like “The Hidden History of Guns and the Second Amendment” and “The Hidden History of Monopolies: How Big Business Destroyed the American Dream,” told AlterNet on Thursday that Trump has a multi-faceted plan to destroy American democracy. It includes sending the military to polling places, seizing ballot boxes, taking unprecedented measures to purge voters from the rolls, seizing on smaller cases of voter fraud — like the one recently revealed in New Jersey, which only led to 400 non-citizen votes since 2023, too small to change election results — and using the post office to stop mail-in voters.

According to Hartmann, Trump is drawing directly from the anti-democracy politics used in the South prior to, during and after the Civil War.

“By the 1840s and early 1850s, even white people who spoke out against the oligarchy or who tried to run against it faced lynching, beatings, and social and economic ostracism,” Hartmann told AlterNet. “The polls were rigged. The elections were — you know, many of these systems, the old jellybean system and everything else — many of these systems that are the precedent for what Trump is proposing were just the law.”

People who opposed the economic oligarchy in America “couldn't win an election.” When marginalized people realized they couldn’t win an election, “the people revolt, essentially. The government is then faced with two choices: they can either back off and say, ‘Okay, no more oligarchy, we'll go back to democracy’ — which is what happened after the Civil War [which started when the South rejected Abraham Lincoln’s election in 1860] — or, it's what happened in 1933 when Franklin Roosevelt was elected president [in 1932]. We had basically become an oligarchy in the 1920s. Roosevelt backed down the oligarchs. He said, ‘They hate me, and I welcome their hatred.’ He literally took them on, and they tried to kill him — in fact, they tried to kidnap him and kill him. So, 80 years ago it was Roosevelt. 80 years before that, it was Lincoln. 80 years before that, it was Jefferson and his allies [defeating the Federalists, who they believed were monarchical, in 1800] — in each case taking on oligarchy, and in each case defeating it.”

He concluded, “The big question this time — 80 years later, 80 years after World War II, pretty much to the year — is: will this time, when we have a government that supports the oligarchy, be followed by a government that confronts it? Will the next government, put into power in two and a half years, be willing to take on the oligarchy? And can they beat the oligarchy? Or, as I said, are the oligarchs confronted with two choices? One is to revert back to democracy. The other is to come down with the iron fist.”

While Hartmann could not predict whether anti-Trump forces will be able to defeat Trump, he was clear about what Trump is planning on doing — that is, coming down the iron fist against opponents.

“Clearly, that is what Trump is planning,” Hartmann wrote. “It's why he's building out ICE. It's why he's put together this rapid reaction force within the National Guard. It's why he and Hegseth have been purging the senior levels of the military of anybody who might object to that. I think the question that's going to determine the survival of democracy in the United States — and consequently, perhaps, democracy around the world — is whether this transitional oligarchy we're in right now will end up as tyranny or will revert to democracy.”

AlterNet then asked Hartmann about whether Democrats are doing enough to prevent Trump from doing this. Earlier on Thursday, MS NOW reported that Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer convened other Democrats for an emergency meeting on how to counter Trump’s various election theft plans.

“I don't know much about what Schumer was planning — probably another strongly worded letter,” Hartmann wrote. “But what I do know is that after the 2024 election, a friend of mine, Greg Palast, a former BBC and Guardian reporter who broke the story in 2000 about how Jeb Bush purged 90,000 Black people off the rolls just weeks before the 2000 election so his brother could win Florida and become president — Greg Palast, who has a master's degree in statistics in addition to his journalism background, and was a student of Milton Friedman's at the University of Chicago, went to the official government statistics, state by state and federal, on who was allowed to vote and who was purged from the voting rolls leading up to and during the 2024 election. What he found was that about four million people who were legally qualified citizen voters were not allowed to vote.”

He added, “About half of them had been purged in these massive voter purges — Georgia was kind of the epicenter of this; Greg made a movie about it called Vigilantes, Inc. The other half were affected by a variety of techniques: Republican poll workers challenging mail-in ballot signatures, people being given provisional ballots instead of real ballots. With a provisional ballot, in most states you have to show up in person at the Secretary of State's office within a week or two in order for your ballot to be counted — it's called being cured. There's a whole bunch of other things too — cutting the hours the polling places were open, moving them around without notice, phoning in bomb threats. You can't say that was the Republican Party, but that happened a lot in 2024 in very critical swing districts for Democrats.”

Hartmann concluded, “He was able to demonstrate — and he's published this — that had those four million people been able to vote (and we now know who those four million people were), Kamala Harris would have been president, and Democrats would control the House and Senate. So it has already worked for them. And this time, they realize it's going to take more than purging four million people — they're going to have to do six, eight, ten million people.”

Overall, the historian concluded that “the question is, will the Democrats be able to turn out enough people, and will Republican voters be sufficiently disheartened by the failures of the Trump administration that an overwhelming blue wave crashes upon our shores? If it turns out to be a tight election, the voter suppression efforts will probably succeed. So we've got a big job ahead of us.”

Speaking with AlterNet in June, two election experts elaborated on the same concerns that Hartmann broke down in the July interview.

“I think there are two things to consider,” Pooja Chaudhuri, Senior Counsel/Deputy Legal Director at Democracy Defenders Fund, told AlterNet. “One is that the election is made up of voters, and so the outcome depends on people turning out to the polls and voting. The problem is ... the chilling effect on voters.”

Chaudhuri continued, “When voters hear that ICE may be deployed to the polls, that mail-in voting rules are changing close to the election, a lot of voters might say, ‘I'm just not going to go out and vote.’ That could happen in many different ways. There are vulnerable communities — people may come from mixed-status families — they're US citizens, but they might decide, "I'm not going to vote." So one aspect is the chilling effect on voters that all of these actions would have.” In addition to that intimidating factor, Chaudhuri argued the gerrymandering and FBI seizure of Fulton County, GA also could keep voters away from the polls.

“We're going to see consequences in terms of voters saying, ‘I don't want the FBI to get my personal ballot in the future,’” Chaudhuri concluded. “So, to sum up, I do agree that putting it all together, it does paint an ominous picture.”

Dan Vicuña, Senior Policy Director for Voting and Fair Representation at Common Cause, said that Trump’s overall plan is to make sure he cannot lose the midterms.

“What they all add up to is a desire to avoid any accountability to the voters in the midterm elections — to ensure, to preordain the outcome of a midterm that he thinks is going to go badly for him,” Vicuña told AlterNet. “We know, from the Big Lie of the 2020 election to spurring on a violent revolt to overthrow a free and fair election, that he has no respect for democratic norms, for the voice of the people. This is entirely about his own power and his own ego. He will even invest in protecting that ego and protecting his power at the expense of the needs of the public. People are suffering with high gas prices and affordability issues, and he does not care. All that matters is protecting his power, and he has no interest in whether he does that through democratic means.”

He continued, “I think this all adds up to a desire to ensure that his party stays in power and his ability to do what he wants — to attack vulnerable communities — remains intact.”
news.summary:
Even though Americans are largely rejecting his election denial conspiracy theories, President Donald Trump does not care because he is not only hoping to steal the 2026 midterms — he plans on turning America into the Confederacy, right down to the white supremacy and lack of democracy, at least according to a prominent historian.

Thom Hartmann, author of books like “The Hidden History of Guns and the Second Amendment” and “The Hidden History of Monopolies: How Big Business Destroyed the American Dream,” told AlterNet on Thursday that Trump has a multi-faceted plan to destroy American democracy. It includes sending the military to polling places, seizing ballot boxes, taking unprecedented measures to purge voters from the rolls, seizing on smaller cases of voter fraud — like the one recently revealed in New Jersey, which only led to 400 non-citizen votes since 2023, too small to change election results — and using the post office to stop mail-in voters.

news.title: Did Hakeem Jeffries Prove That Democrats Are Panicking About the Midterms?
news.text:
Rep. Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) wants you to believe Democrats want to fight fraud. Talk about a bizarre flip-flop mere months before the midterm elections.

Something is clearly up.

When President Donald Trump returned to the White House last year and put Elon Musk in charge of the Department of Government Efficiency, Democrats erupted in outrage. Rooting out waste and fraud in federal spending should have been an easy bipartisan win. Instead, the Democrat Party treated it like an assault on the republic and fought DOGE at every turn.

That opposition wasn't just rhetorical. Just last month, the House passed H.Res.1335, a resolution condemning those who defraud the federal government and backing reforms to prevent improper payments, including verifying eligibility before taxpayer money goes out the door. Easy thing to support, right? It should have been. But instead, a total of 177 Democrats voted against it. And guess who was one of them? That’s right: Hakeem Jeffries.

“And our promise to the American people is that we will hold the crooks accountable," he said. "If you’re crook in the United States of America, and you’ve been stealing from the American people, we’re coming after you.”

Democrat House leader Hakeem Jeffries claims that Democrats will now fight fraud:“We will hold the crooks accountable…If you have been stealing from the American people, we’re coming after you.”Didn't you vote against condemning fraud against U.S. government? pic.twitter.com/wBC7i8MZ3P— RNC Research (@RNCResearch) July 23, 2026

It's a nice line. Does anyone really believe he’s being genuine here? I certainly don’t. Nobody's buying what Jeffries is selling. Democrats spent the last year blocking, delaying, and demonizing this administration's efforts to root out waste, fraud, and abuse, and everybody knows exactly why. Something is telling Jeffries and his party that their strategy has become political poison. So, this is what they do. They adopt a position they don’t truly hold because they think it will fool voters.

They do this all the time. Remember when leftists crapped on President Donald Trump’s No Tax on Tips plan, blasting it from all angles, and then gushed when Kamala Harris rehashed the exact same policy on the campaign trail? Democrats want to convince voters they’ll act like Republicans in order to get elected.

But the funny thing is, this shouldn’t even be a partisan issue. The American people don't like watching their tax dollars get stolen, and Democrats have spent a year rabidly defending the people doing the stealing and defrauding. Make no mistake about it, this 180 has something to do with what their polling is telling them.

Recommended: Is This the Reason Your Leftist Relatives Are Cutting You Off?

It would be nice to believe Democrats have seen the light on fighting fraud, but they’ve given us no reason to trust them. If they really want to prove they’re serious, they can condemn the fraudsters and support the administration’s effort to root out fraud.

And while they’re at it, there’s another 80/20 issue they can flip on.

As we all know, Democrats have blocked commonsense legislation like the SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to vote and a valid photo ID at the polls. Both ideas are wildly popular with the American people. In fact, the politics are so lopsided that even Democrat strategist James Carville is telling his own party to cave. "We are not going to win the war against some kind of ID," Carville said on his podcast. "It doesn't make any sense, it doesn't fix anything, but maybe you just go along with it and move to the next thing."

💥NEW: James Carville calls on Dems to *PASS* national voter ID law💥"We are NOT gonna win the war against some kind of a ID ... EVERY poll shows 75% or better say we should have some kind of a ID to vote ... just quit fighting and go along with it!" pic.twitter.com/rrjkJlHKiU— Jason Cohen 🇺🇸 (@JasonJournoDC) July 23, 2026

It’s funny how so much of what Republicans are fighting for is popular ideas, isn’t it? That’s why Democrats pretend to adopt those ideas as their own. But let's be honest here: if Jeffries and his party actually wanted to prove they care about fighting fraud, whether it’s connected to government spending or the ballot box, they'd start voting for the measures already in front of them. But they won’t. Why? Because they just want to win an election.

Editor’s Note: The 2026 Midterms will determine the fate of President Trump’s America First agenda. Republicans must maintain control of both chambers of Congress.

news.title: GOP Rep. Mike Lawler leads Dem challenger Cait Conley in latest NY-17 poll
news.text:
A key congressional battleground race in the Hudson Valley is heating up with Rep. Mike Lawler (R-NY) holding a slim 5-point lead over his Dem challenger in a new poll. 

The survey from polling outfit co/efficient released Thursday shows Lawler with 50% of the vote to Cait Conley’s 45% as the two jockey for position in one of the most competitive House races in the country.

Lawler had an even wider lead amongst independents with 54% to Conley’s 39%. The poll has a 2.7% margin of error. 

“We anticipate Mike will be ‘surging’ past Cait on Election Night as he heads towards a third term of delivering for Hudson Valley voters,” Lawler spokesperson Ciro Riccardi told The Post.

“Fortunately for Cait, she can go back to her lucrative consulting for AI defense contractors, maybe even helping advise them on who to deport next,” he further snarked, pointing to Conley’s work after serving on the National Security Council under President Joe Biden.

While Cook Political Report rates the 17th District as a toss-up, Fox News’ rankings moved the pivotal race into the “lean Dem” column this week.

“Though the choice seems obvious, Cait is not taking a single vote for granted,” Conley spokesperson Maddy Rosen wrote in a statement to The Post.

“While Mike Lawler spends his days cozying up to the corporations and lobbyists backing his campaign, Cait is crisscrossing the district talking to working families about the soaring utility rates and healthcare cuts they’ve experienced on Mike Lawler’s watch.”

Lawler handily defeated former lefty Rep. Mondaire Jones by six points in 2024, despite the district going blue for Kamala Harris.

The suburban district includes all or parts of Westchester, Putnam and Dutchess counties.
news.summary: Lawler had an even wider lead amongst independents with 54% to Conley’s 39%. The poll has a 2.7% margin of error.
news.author: Vaughn Golden

news.title: Trump's Latest Burn Of Jon Ossoff Is Straight Out Of His Playbook — And It's Embarrassing
news.text:
President Donald Trump’s insults are an almost inextricable part of his brand at this point. While his most recent insult leveled at Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Ga.) didn’t totally stick the landing — it did show off something telling. 

On Wednesday, Trump, attempting to once again call Ossoff “Pee Wee Herman” (like the Paul Reubens character, as he did late last month), instead repeatedly called him “Pinky Herman.” (And was laughed at a bunch.)

“Your terrible senator, Ossoff, he’s terrible,” Trump said in the speech at a rally in Georgia. “And the Democrats in Congress are voting with the communists every single time. They’re afraid of the communists.”

“I fought Ossoff, you know, we won,” he continued, seemingly referring to the Georgia special election nearly a decade ago. “We’ve had a great success against this guy. He reminds me of Pinky Herman. Did you ever see Pinky Herman?” 

The Pee Wee/Pinky nickname might be considered an upgrade to some, including Ossoff, who previously critiqued Trump’s more vulgar attempt at a nickname — Jon Os(jerk!)off — claiming he “didn’t think it was his best work as nicknames go” and saying the “globally humiliated” president was “increasingly unstable.”

This is very on brand, as far as Trump insults go. 

Trump’s nicknames and insults can often fall into different categories — we’ve seen him insult people for their supposedly “low IQ,” monosyllabic tabloid taglines like “sick” and, of course, ample comments about others’ looks.

His nicknames are often “derisive adjectives,” as Edwin Battistella, linguist and author of “Dangerous Crooked Scoundrels: Insulting the President from Washington to Trump,” told HuffPost, listing off familiar ones like “Liddle Marco” (Rubio), “Crooked Hillary” (Clinton) and “Sleepy Joe” (Biden). 

Battistella said that this particular insult is “interesting, because it suggests that Trump misspoke” but offers a more generous interpretation, based on Trump’s other contextual comments, that it “raises the possibility that he was trying to suggest that Democrats were Communists, reviving the ‘pinko’ insult of the past.” 

Battistella says “Trump often goes to comparison” as inspiration for his insults and noted that these comparisons were “based in somewhat dated pop culture.” 

Battistella also mentioned the separate occasions Trump referred to both Texas Senate candidate James Talarico and former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg as “Alfred E. Neuman,” as “similarly mired in vintage popular culture” and noted that they aren’t necessarily gunning for accuracy. 

“As is the case when Trump used adjectives, the comparisons are a stretch,” he said. “He is trying to belittle rather than present an accurate characterization.”

“Ironically, Trump’s opponents have portrayed him as Alfred E. Neuman,” he also noted, “and his assistant chief of staff has been characterized as “Pee Wee German.”

There is another weird pattern that’s hard to ignore. 

When you have such plentiful data on insults from one individual, it’s hard not to notice certain patterns. This insult (and the Alfred E. Neuman ones) were notably leveled at white men. 

“Trump does seem to use different types of insults when referring to white men than when insulting women and people of color,” Battistella said, noting attacks on Barack Obama, Kamala Harris, Hakeem Jeffries and Jasmine Crockett’s intelligence over the years, referring to them as “low IQ” and “not smart.” 

While he’s occasionally used this same line of insult on opponents like Tim Walz, the racist trend does still stand. 

“So there seems to be a pattern there indeed,” he said. 

The insult certainly won’t bother Ossoff. So what’s the point? 

Ossoff’s previous response to Trump’s direct mockery was relatively indifferent. It’s hard to imagine this one — botched delivery and all — will get much more out of him. 

But research from March 2025 by the Polarization Research Lab at Dartmouth College might offer insights into the actual endgame of these insults. 

The researchers identified politicians who repeatedly use personal attacks on their peers — who they call “conflict entrepreneurs” — and tracked their behavior over 2.2 million public statements from the 118th U.S. Congress (Jan. 3, 2023 to Jan. 3, 2025). 

They found that using these outcomes saw “no corresponding positive relationship with fundraising, vote margins, legislative success, or personal wealth” nor did it have “correlation between a legislator’s use of insults and the partisan animosity in their district,” per the research.

Instead of this more “traditional” success, researchers identified a very different victory for the most combative members of Congress: Media attention. They found that the more often a politician used these personal attacks, the less likely they were to engage in their actual jobs — discussing actual policy, co-sponsoring legislation, or gaining assignments to committees. 

“These findings suggest that politicians are using the attacks as a strategy to become part of the national political debate without relying on conventional means of legislative work and policymaking,” Marc Jacob, assistant professor of democracy and global affairs at the University of Notre Dame and one of the study’s co-authors, said in a press release about the study. 

Jacob went on to note that the media environment and increased attention on personal attacks over policy “incentivizes people to engage in incivility if the only way to break through is with insults.”

So while it doesn’t seem likely that these attacks will get Trump any closer to his professed policy goals, it can certainly be seen as a cry for attention.
news.author: Katherine Speller

news.title: Hasan Piker chooses AOC as his preferred 2028 presidential pick, says she’s ‘the only’ potential candidate he likes
news.text:
Far-left Twitch streamer Hasan Piker selected Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., over a slate of prominent Democrats as his preferred 2028 presidential candidate on Wednesday — arguing that AOC could excite the Democratic Party’s base and bring infrequent voters into a winning coalition.

When asked to choose between Ocasio-Cortez and Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., as his preferred candidate, Piker immediately selected the New York congresswoman.

“AOC. It’s not even a question,” Piker said during a Zeteo interview with Mehdi Hasan.

Piker made the choice at the end of a rapid-fire exercise that began with California Gov. Gavin Newsom and former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel. The list later included former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg, former Vice President Kamala Harris, Sen. Jon Ossoff, D-Ga., Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker, Sen. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md., and Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif.

Piker said Ocasio-Cortez stood apart from the other potential contenders presented to him.

“AOC is, out of all of the people that you mentioned,” Piker said. “AOC is the only one that I would like.”

Piker acknowledged that her race and gender could present electoral obstacles but rejected the argument that they would make victory impossible.

“These are hurdles, yes,” Piker said. “But these are hurdles that one can clear.”

Piker argued Ocasio-Cortez could overcome those barriers by campaigning with conviction and reaching voters who do not regularly participate in elections.

“As long as you have moral conviction, as long as you can excite the base, as long as you can actually create a unique coalition and activate low-propensity voters,” Piker said. “Which I think AOC has the capacity to do, it would be a fantastic left candidate.”

He compared Ocasio-Cortez favorably with Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., whose presidential campaign helped elevate democratic socialism within the party.

“It would be a fantastic left candidate, even better than Bernie in many ways,” Piker said.

Piker also offered criticism of Ocasio-Cortez’s political presentation. He said she had been effective on social media and willing to take progressive positions in Congress but argued she could appear too concerned with optics.

“The way she carries herself, I think, is a little bit too, like, optics-driven, too liberal and too optics-driven,” Piker said. “I think she doesn’t harness the populist rage in the way that she communicates.”

Fox News Digital reached out to Ocasio-Cortez for comment but did not immediately hear back.

Ocasio-Cortez has not announced a 2028 campaign and said in May that she had not decided whether to run for president. She had increased her national travel, endorsements and campaign appearances, including a recent Michigan stop for progressive Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed.
news.summary: The far-left Twitch streamer said Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez could activate low-propensity voters and excite the Democratic base.
news.author: Fox News

news.title: Pete Buttigieg, JD Vance at top of new 2028 presidential primary poll
news.text:
Although the midterms are still months away, potential candidates for the 2028 presidential election are starting to float to the top, with former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg and Vice President JD Vance leading their respective primaries.

The newest poll from Emerson College, released on Thursday, July 23, shows Buttigieg leading among Democratic voters with 19%, with California Gov. Gavin Newsom at 17% and Georgia Sen. Jon Ossoff and New York Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez tied at 13%.

On the Republican side, it's a very tight two-horse race, as Vance leads with 39%, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio coming in with 38% of GOP primary voters. In a distant third is Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis with only 5%, while 9% of voters are undecided.

According to Emerson College, the poll was conducted between July 19 and July 20 among 1,100 likely voters. The margin of error of Thursday’s poll is plus or minus 2.9 percentage points.

A relatively quiet Trump

With such a seemingly tight race for the GOP nomination in 2028, President Donald Trump has so far declined to endorse a successor.

However, the president did praise both Vance and Rubio as a potential “perfect ticket” during a May 11 White House event.

“I do believe that’s a dream team, but these are minor details. That does not mean you have my endorsement under any circumstances,” Trump said. “I think it sounds like presidential candidate and vice presidential candidate.”

A fluid polling scene

Buttigieg and Vance’s leads in Thursday’s poll come a month after the same pollster published a poll in June in which the two candidates led their respective primaries.

However, outside of Emerson College polls, both candidates have seen differing performances.

An AtlasIntel poll conducted May 4 to May 7 found Ocasio-Cortez leading Democratic candidates at 26%, followed by Buttigieg and Newsom. Former Vice President Kamala Harris ranked fourth, with roughly half of Ocasio-Cortez’s support.

On the Republican side, in the same AtlasIntel poll, Rubio led with about 45% of primary voters, while Vance and DeSantis trailed behind.

Then, a McLaughlin & Associates survey, released June 25, showed Harris leading Democratic voters at 26%, ahead of Newsom at 16% and Ocasio-Cortez at 9%. On the Republican side, Vance held a wider lead at 35%, compared with Rubio's 15%.

More: AOC responds as polls put her among Democrats’ top picks for 2028

Bad news for Republicans and Trump?

Apart from looking ahead to 2028, Thursday’s poll also had possible bad news for the GOP ahead of November’s midterms as the party seeks to maintain its hold on Congress.

According to the Emerson College poll, Democrats led Republicans by 11% in a generic congressional ballot. 

“Driving the Democrats’ significant lead on the ballot are women voters, who break for the Democratic candidate by a 27-point margin, 56% to 39%, while men are more split: 49% support the Democrat and 44% the Republican,” Spencer Kimball, executive director of Emerson College Polling, said in a news release.

Democrats’ strong performance also comes as Trump remains deeply unpopular with Americans, with the poll finding that only 39% approve of his time in office.

Fernando Cervantes Jr. is a trending news reporter for USA TODAY. Reach him at fernando.cervantes@usatodayco.com and follow him on X @fern_cerv_.
news.author: Usa Today

news.title: WATCH: AOC won't rule out White House bid after poll names her Democrats' top 2028 pick
news.text: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., is keeping the door open for a 2028 White House bid after a new poll showed the New York Democrat as the leading choice among prospective Democratic presidential candidates. She sidestepped Fox News Digital’s question asking if she would like to be the Democratic 2028 nominee. "I have genuinely not really weighed seriously anything past the midterm elections," Ocasio-Cortez told Fox News Digital when asked if she is interested in a run. "I think that I'm taking in this information just like anybody else." HASAN PIKER MAKES AOC HIS 2028 PICK FOR PRESIDENT, SAYS IT'S 'NOT EVEN A QUESTION’ The progressive House Democrat repeatedly has toyed with the idea of both a presidential bid and a potential Senate bid to run against Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, D-N.Y., when he is up for re-election in two years. Insight into the Democratic Party’s top pick for the presidential election in 2028 was released in a University of New Hampshire Granite State Poll Wednesday showing Ocasio-Cortez as voters' frontrunner candidate. While New Hampshire no longer holds the first official Democratic presidential primary, the state has played an influential role in testing White House hopefuls and remains closely watched for early voter signs. Ocasio-Cortez dodged giving her own thoughts on the poll that shows strong interest in her to lead the Democrats in the next election cycle. WATCH: AOC WON'T RULE OUT SENATE BID AFTER NEW YORK PROGRESSIVES NOTCH PRIMARY WINS: 'INSPIRED AND ENCOURAGED' "You're gonna have to ask a voter about that," Ocasio-Cortez said when asked about the new poll. "I have less insight into it than they do." The poll projected her ahead of other prospective candidates that are likely to run in 2028, including Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg, former Vice President Kamala Harris, Gov. Gavin Newsom and other prominent party members. Support for Ocasio-Cortez rose to 22% among likely Democratic voters. Ocasio-Cortez gained seven points since February's survey, placing her one point ahead of Buttigieg and 17 points ahead of Harris. Both Harris and Newsom lost ground since February’s survey. BERNIE SANDERS, DSA REVEAL DEMANDS FOR DEM PARTY AFTER SOCIALISTS SWEEP NEW YORK ELECTIONS Ocasio-Cortez has endorsed many socialists who won in their respective primary elections, beating out incumbents and mainstream Democrats. Ocasio-Cortez has been endorsed by the Democratic Socialists of America in previous campaigns. She is largely expected to receive a Democratic Socialists of America endorsement for the 2028 election as infighting has erupted within the organization over its potential pick. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Some Democratic Socialists of America members are advocating announcing an earlier presidential endorsement because the organization usually selects its endorsement at its biennial national convention in summer 2027. Fox News Digital’s Paul Steinhauser contributed to this report.
news.summary: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez sidestepped questions about a 2028 presidential bid after a new poll showed her leading all Democratic presidential candidates.
news.author: Hannah Brennan

news.title: List of Lawmakers Joining Paris Hilton to Back DEFIANCE Act: What to Know
news.text: Paris Hilton and a bipartisan group of House lawmakers are pushing legislation aimed at combating sexually explicit artificial intelligence (AI)-generated deepfakes amid growing concerns over the spread of nonconsensual digital forgeries online. The effort, introduced by Democratic Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, has drawn support from dozens of Republicans and Democrats who say victims need stronger legal tools to hold creators and distributors of the content accountable. Despite attracting both bipartisan and celebrity support, the measure has seen little movement in the chamber since being introduced in May 2025. Meanwhile, the Senate unanimously passed its version of the legislation in January of this year. Newsweek has reached out to representatives for Hilton as well as Ocasio-Cortez, Republican Representative Laurel Lee and several of the other cosponsors for comment via email on Thursday. What Is the DEFIANCE Act? The Disrupt Explicit Forged Images and Non-Consensual Edits (DEFIANCE) Act introduced by Ocasio-Cortez in May 2025 would create a federal civil remedy for victims of nonconsensual sexually explicit deepfakes and other intimate digital forgeries. The legislation is aimed at images or videos that use AI or other technology to falsely depict an identifiable person in nude or sexual content without their consent. Under the bill, victims would be able to sue individuals who create, distribute or threaten to distribute such material and seek damages in court. Victims could recover damages of up to $250,000 in cases connected to sexual assault, stalking or harassment. Supporters say the legislation is intended to address a rapidly growing problem, as AI tools make it easier to create realistic fake images and videos. Lawmakers behind the bill argue that victims can suffer reputational harm, emotional distress, harassment and other consequences even when viewers know the images are fabricated. The bill has 57 cosponsors. Speaking at a January news conference, Hilton reflected on the release of a sexually explicit video recorded when she was 19 and leaked by an older, former boyfriend, saying: “People called it a scandal. It wasn’t. It was abuse.” She continued, “There were no laws at the time to protect me. There weren’t even words for what had been done to me. ... They sold my pain for clicks, and then they told me to be quiet, to move on, to even be grateful for the attention. These people didn’t see me as a young woman who had been exploited.” Lee, of Florida, said at the news conference, "Victims have seen their reputations destroyed, their careers derailed, their mental health profoundly affected, and for too long, our laws have lagged behind this technology. Victims have been left without clear, consistent legal tools when their life is stolen and weaponized without their consent. The DEFIANCE Act closes that gap." Ocasio-Cortez mentioned her personal experience while speaking about the legislation in January, saying, “As a survivor of sexual assault myself, this resurfaces trauma for so many people across the country, and that is what it is intended to do,” she said about nonconsensual explicit deepfakes. “Because the creation of this content parallels the same exact intention of physical assault, which is about power, domination and humiliation. And while these images may be digital, the harm to victims is very real. Women lose their jobs when they are targeted with this. Teenagers switch schools and children lose their lives. Congress has a moral obligation to stop this harm," she added. Last year, Congress passed the Take It Down Act, which created criminal penalties for sharing nonconsensual intimate images, including sexually explicit deepfakes. President Donald Trump signed the legislation, which first lady Melania Trump backed. The DEFIANCE Act works to take the previous legislation further. Why Is It Stalled? Despite bipartisan support, the measure has not yet reached the House floor for a vote and the reason remains unclear. One theory, from four people involved in the legislation who spoke with Politico, is that some lawmakers' reluctance to advance the bill stemmed from its association with Ocasio-Cortez, who is viewed as a potential progressive contender for the 2028 presidential nomination, and other Congress members not wanting to boost her profile even more. Ocasio-Cortez has not announced a presidential bid, although several polls find her leading a hypothetical Democratic primary. A new University of New Hampshire Survey Center poll found her leading the pack with 22 percent, compared to former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg's 21 percent, Arizona Senator Mark Kelly's 9 percent, California Governor Gavin Newsom's 8 percent, Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders' 6 percent and former Vice President Kamala Harris' 5 percent. The poll surveyed 648 likely 2028 Democratic presidential primary voters from July 15 to July 20 and has a margin of error of 3.8 percent. The progressive Democrat said earlier in July that the reason for the holdup is Republican Representative Jim Jordan, chair of the Judiciary Committee. She told The Independent, “Senator [Lindsey] Graham, Senator [Josh] Hawley as well were instrumental in securing bipartisan support for this legislation to protect kids, women, everyone from non-consensual AI-developed porn, and it passed the Senate unanimously. Every single Republican voted for it, and so when it comes to the House, I think we just got to ask Jim Jordan.” The legislation was referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary in May 2025. The committee chair controls the panel's agenda, including which bills receive hearings and markups. Newsweek has reached out to Jordan and other House leaders for comment via email on Thursday. Although the DEFIANCE Act has attracted bipartisan support, debates over AI-generated content and other related legislation have prompted some digital-rights advocates to warn that lawmakers should ensure new regulations are narrowly tailored and include safeguards for lawful expression and due process. Which Republicans Cosponsor the DEFIANCE Act? Which Democrats Cosponsor the DEFIANCE Act? For now, supporters, including Hilton, are continuing to press for House action as they seek to move the legislation to the president's desk. Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Edward T. Cummins.
news.summary: Paris Hilton and bipartisan House members are pushing the DEFIANCE Act to help victims of AI deepfakes.
news.author: Mandy Taheri

news.title: Why Democrats Can’t Do Better Than Kamala Harris in 2028
news.text:
There are only two lanes to the Democratic presidential nomination two years from now, and one of them is already blocked—by Kamala Harris.

Get ready for the Kamala comeback, followed, in all likelihood, by a replay of her crushing defeat by the Republican nominee. Believe it or not, the ex-VP occupies what counts as the “center lane” within the party, and for most of the last three decades that’s the one that’s led to the nomination.

Republicans used to be the party of orderly succession, even if the candidate whose turn had come looked like a certain loser: think Bob Dole in ’96. But recently, it’s been Democrats who’ve tended to pick a party elder as their nominee: Al Gore, as a sitting VP, might not have been remarkable in 2000, but in ’04 it was John Kerry, in ’16 Hillary Clinton and in 2020 Joe Biden—with only Barack Obama butting ahead of the line in 2008.

Democrats like a known quantity, a reliable liberal who isn’t too overtly radical; the radicalism can wait until after Election Day. So they’re leaning to running Harris again, even if donors aren’t happy about it: They blew nearly $2 billion on her campaign and aligned PAC’s in 2024, only for Harris to lose every battleground state to Trump.
news.author: Beege Welborn

news.title: New Hampshire poll gives 2 Democrats early edge in 2028 race
news.text:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., and former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg lead a hypothetical 2028 Democratic presidential primary field in New Hampshire, according to a new poll.

The results, released Wednesday, offer an early snapshot of a contest that remains more than two years away. The University of New Hampshire Survey Center found Ocasio-Cortez with 22% support among likely Democratic primary voters in the state, narrowly ahead of Buttigieg at 21%. 

The poll, which surveyed 596 likely Democratic primary voters in New Hampshire from July 15 to 20, also found that Ocasio-Cortez performed strongest among self-described socialists and progressives, while Buttigieg attracted support from a broader mix of progressives, liberals and moderates.

Both candidates have fueled (or at least have not thrown cold water on) speculation about possible 2028 presidential bids. While campaigning for progressive candidates in Democratic primaries last weekend, Ocasio-Cortez told Politico, “I mean, I can’t say that I’ve ruled it out,” when asked about running. Buttigieg struck a similar tone during a July 13 visit to Iowa, answering, “Nope,” when asked whether he would rule out a presidential campaign.

No other potential candidates in the poll reached double digits. Sen. Mark Kelly of Arizona received 9%, California Gov. Gavin Newsom 8%, Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont 6% and former Vice President Kamala Harris 5%.

Any prognostication about the 2028 race, however, comes with significant caveats: No major Democratic candidate has formally entered the race, the first nominating contests are still years away, and presidential primary polling this far in advance is not a sure indicator of electoral success, as candidates enter and exit races, gain national exposure and campaign across states.

The Democratic National Committee stripped New Hampshire of its first-in-the-nation primary status for the 2024 cycle after voting to make South Carolina the party’s first nominating contest. 

The change, championed by then-President Joe Biden, was intended to give a more racially diverse electorate greater influence in selecting the Democratic nominee after Biden credited South Carolina’s voters with reviving his 2020 presidential campaign. The DNC has not yet finalized its 2028 presidential nominating calendar, which leaves New Hampshire’s role in the next Democratic primary process uncertain. New Hampshire remains the first-in-the-nation primary on the GOP side.

Some critics have long cautioned that the Granite State is not fully representative of the national Democratic electorate. New Hampshire has a smaller, disproportionately white electorate relative to the party’s nationwide coalition, meaning candidates who perform well there do not necessarily maintain that advantage as the primary calendar moves to more racially and geographically diverse states.
news.author: Erum Salam

news.title: Steve Hilton battles Gavin Newsom, Kamala Harris and the ‘Machine’ in new AI-driven campaign ad
news.text:
Republican Steve Hilton is bringing AI warfare to California’s governor’s race, releasing a cinematic campaign ad Thursday that portrays Gov. Gavin Newsom, former Vice President Kamala Harris and the state’s Democratic establishment as operating a giant political “machine” that spits out Xavier Becerra as the state’s next governor.

The spot was produced by Charles Curran — the brainchild behind a viral “Pratt-Man” video featuring reality television personality Spencer Pratt during his unsuccessful campaign for Los Angeles mayor. 

The roughly two-minute Hilton spot — titled “Machine” — blends artificial intelligence-generated animation with live campaign footage to depict California’s Democratic leadership manufacturing Becerra as Newsom’s successor while ordinary Californians struggle with soaring taxes, high gas prices and homelessness. 

“This ad is showing what this is all about — the people against the corrupt machine in Sacramento,” Hilton told The Post.

It comes just a day after his call for support from anti-Trump voters.

“Our government has been hijacked by a group of special interests and regular Californians have been losing out.”

The ad opens with a narrator declaring, “As long as the machine leaves California, nothing will ever change,” before cutting to a woman cradling a baby who says, “I don’t know if we can continue living here.”

It then shifts to an AI-generated boardroom where animated versions of Newsom, Harris and political fat cats gather around a table. Figures representing “nonprofits,” “Chamber of Commerce” and “government unions” sit nearby as Newsom tells the group, “Okay, we got to figure out this governor’s thing. I’d do it forever, but it’s my turn to be president.”

“Bring in the machine,” Newsom says. The machine flashes the name Becerra.

“The Biden guy who lost all those kids,” Newsom says.

“That can’t be right. Run it again,” Harris replies.

Newsom then describes it as “the thing we created to make sure nothing ever changes.” he adds, “As long as you submit to the machine, you don’t have to do anything.”

Becerra, realizing the task at hand, smiles and says: “Nothing. I can do that.”

The ad later depicts Becerra being shouted down by an angry crowd before being pelted with an object, then pivoting to Hilton speaking before cheering supporters.

“Who else is tired of the highest taxes in the country, the highest gas prices in the country, the worst homelessness in the country?” Hilton asks. “This is our state, not the machines. That’s what we’re fighting for!”

In the closing sequence, protesters storm what appears to be the Capitol rotunda, taking on robots in suits, before a firefighter tosses Hilton a California flag, which he plants into the front of the giant machine.

“Time to take California back!” Hilton shouts.

The ad ends back inside the fictional boardroom, where Harris turns to Newsom and asks, “Can we get Swalwell back?”

Hilton said early reaction to the video has been overwhelmingly positive.

“I’m getting texts from people who say they love it,” he said. “It’s the kind of energy we need.”

The commercial also marks Hilton’s embrace of the viral AI-generated political videos that have become an increasingly visible feature of California campaigns.

“Of course Steve is playing to his Trump-loving MAGA base — the only folks who can stand to watch an ad with him in it,” said Jonathan Underland, Becerra’s spokesperson.

“Force-feeding voters AI slop didn’t play out well for Spencer, and it won’t work for Steve,“ he added. 

Unlike those videos, Hilton said, the new ad was produced directly for his campaign.

With Newsom barred by term limits from seeking another term, the race will determine who leads California beginning next year.

Hilton said voters should expect more unconventional AI-enhanced campaign ads.

“This is just the first,” he said. “A lot more to come.”
news.summary: Republican Steve Hilton is bringing AI warfare to California’s governor’s race, releasing a cinematic campaign ad Thursday that portrays Gov. Gavin Newsom, former Vice President Kamala Harris and the state’s Democratic establishment as operating a giant political “machine” that spits out Xavier Becerra as the state’s next governor. The spot was produced by Charles Curran...
news.author: Josh Koehn

news.title: AOC inherits the Bernie Sanders lane for the Democratic presidential primaries
news.text:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) has inherited the socialist lane for Democratic presidential primaries from Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT), and the country should be prepared to face that fact in the 2028 elections.

A recently released University of New Hampshire poll put Ocasio-Cortez’s support in the Granite State at 22%, just ahead of former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg’s 21%. This resembles the 2020 Democratic primary results in New Hampshire, where Sanders defeated Buttigieg 25%-24%. More notably, Ocasio-Cortez has seen forward momentum in the state, with University of New Hampshire polling showing her jump from 14% in October 2025 and 15% in February 2026 to the 22% at which she sits now.

This does not necessarily mean that Ocasio-Cortez will be the nominee. Former Vice President Kamala Harris is still the polling favorite, and would benefit the most if Democrats keep South Carolina at the front of the primary calendar. Gov. Gavin Newsom (D-CA) is the most likely nominee, and he would benefit the most if Nevada is placed at the front of the calendar, as he led a November 2025 poll over a second-place Buttigieg 37%-19%. Harris or Newsom (or Buttigieg) would represent the “establishment” side of the primary, which prevailed over Sanders in both 2016 and 2020.
