news.title: MAGA declaring war on seniors — and it could be their downfall
news.text:
According to data from the Roper Center at Cornell University, 50 percent of U.S. seniors — those who are 65 or older — voted for Donald Trump in 2024 compared to 49 percent for Democratic presidential nominee Kamala Harris. The senior vote was close in 2024, and seniors, Roper says, played an important role in getting Trump past the finish line. But according to Salon's Heather Digby Parton, seniors could be the ones who doom Republicans in the 2026 midterms.

Parton notes that because so many seniors vote GOP, "it's always been a mystery" to her "why Republicans have always been so bent on destroying the safety net that brought the elderly out of poverty and assured them a baseline level of subsistence and medical care." And she points that crucial safety-net programs used by seniors — Social Security and Medicare — came from liberal Democratic presidents.

"From the moment Franklin D. Roosevelt signed the Social Security Act in 1935 to the day Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Medicare and Medicaid Act 30 years later," Parton explains, "the right has been trying to destroy them. In the early days, it sprang from their obsession with anti-communism, arguments that the country couldn't afford it, and that individuals and their families are rightfully responsible for their own care in old age. In a 1961 radio address — 20 years before he became president — Ronald Reagan said, 'One of the traditional methods of imposing statism or socialism on a people has been by way of medicine' ... Before Medicare was created, only 54 percent of elderly people had health insurance, and that could be cancelled without cause when they got sick — which, as you know, tends to happen when you get old." 

Parton continues, "Three years after it was enacted, 96 percent of people over 65 had hospital insurance. It was literally a life saver. Over the years, Medicaid, created at the same time to assist people living below the poverty line, came to cover many of the health expenses that Medicare didn't for elderly people, most importantly for nursing homes and caregivers. The GOP has been hacking away at these vital programs ever since, and yet, senior citizens have been voting for them in greater numbers than any other age cohort."

The Salon journalist stresses, however, that seniors could rebel against Republicans in a big way if they think the Trump administration is hurting Social Security and Medicare.

"The Republican war on the elderly has taken an even darker — and potentially painful and even deadly — turn, and not just for seniors below the poverty line," Parton observes. "Trump's immigration policies are drastically shrinking the pool of workers who care for the elderly in assisted-living facilities and in their own homes ... This could well be an issue that cuts across party lines."

Parton continues, "Any older person who is told that their caregiver is being deported because of Trump's policies could likewise be angry and appalled ... There are a lot of seniors, and they vote. Republicans should think long and hard about making these people their adversaries."

news.title: DAVID MARCUS: In Middle America, socialist politics ages like milk
news.text: Erick was here for a conference on transportation technology when I talked to him about his hometown of Denver, and how socialist congressional candidate Melat Kiros recently won a shocking Democratic primary over her more moderate rival. "I’m a Democrat," Erick told me. "I lean a bit to the Republican side maybe, but these socialists are crazy." In his 40s, having lived for 15 years in England, Erick is socially liberal, but also patriotic. He has concerns about AI and data centers, but also sees their utility. In short, he is exactly the kind of Democratic voter that is thinking twice about his affiliation with the party amid the rise of Marxism within. I asked how long before Election Day Erick had even heard of Kiros and her commie candidacy. "A week, maybe?" he said. "It was fast, and I’m even involved in the very local politics on the homeowner level. She just came out of nowhere." This is a repeating pattern now for the far-left socialist congressional candidates across the country. Nobody in New York, or beyond, knew who Darializa Chevalier was about two weeks before she knocked off longtime Rep. Adriano Espailat. Same for likely future member of Congress Claire Valdez. This is the same model that was used by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani in his 2025 primary race, and by Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in 2018 when she knocked off Rep. Joe Crowley. It has three steps. First, a 24/7, 365-day-a-year organizing machine that harnesses and harvests votes for the socialist party. Second, the use of non-traditional media to introduce the wacky candidate to the equally wacky activist class. Third, a mainstream introduction with very little time left to kick the socialist tires. This is exactly how, by the time Erick had ever heard of Milot Kiros, she had all but already won. As successful as this socialist model has been in congressional races across the nation, Senate contests are proving to be a tougher nut to crack, in large part because they require candidates like Adbul El-Sayed in Michigan to stay in the spotlight for an extended period. That would be the same spotlight that melted former Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner. It brings to mind the presidential candidacy of Kamala Harris, which she says she lost because 107 days wasn’t enough time to campaign. In fact, it was probably too long to campaign; she might have won a 15-day race, and that is exactly the type the DSA likes to run. As Terry, who is in her 30s and a Detroit native, told me of El-Sayed, "There keep being these crazy things he said in the past, they keep coming," a fact that even CNN’s Jake Tapper acknowledged in an interview with El-Sayed about his refusal to celebrate the death of Iran’s ayatollah, which went so badly for the candidate that El-Sayed is now trashing CNN at his rallies. The biggest hope now for El-Sayed’s opponent Rep. Haley Stevens is that he has peaked too soon. Her ads are hitting the airwaves at a frantic pace in an effort to make that so. When I arrived in Detroit last week, there were still barely any TV ads airing. By the day I left, Stevens ads were thick on screens everywhere, news channels, sports channels, the Hallmark channel, you name it. The theme of these ads, beyond just saying that El-Sayed is not electable, are his previous comments attacking both former President Barack Obama and current Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer, neither of whom have endorsed in the race. The message is clear: El-Sayed is not a normal Democrat, but a radical, and Stevens still has two weeks left to make this charge stick as Abdul insists he isn’t a socialist while sharing the stage with Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., and AOC. Unlike Erick in Colorado, who had the sudden success of socialist Kiros thrust upon him before he even knew it was happening, Michigan voters have had time to examine El-Sayed and still have time to do so. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP It raises an important question for our current political moment: Are the Marxists of the Democratic Socialists of America even trying to win the votes of Democrats like Erick, or are they simply trying to blindside them? The sneaky, last-minute surprise approach has done well for the communists on the congressional level, but if they want to win statewide, or nationwide, they need to win American voters over, not simply pull a fast one on them. news.summary: Socialist candidates like Melat Kiros and Darializa Chevalier are winning primaries using a last-minute surprise model that blindsides voters.

news.title: Disaster for Trump: Fox News warns of 'end days for GOP'
news.text:
A pair of new polls revealed the true extent of the damage President Donald Trump has done to his party’s midterm chances, and according to one leading political reporter, it amounts to the “end days for GOP.”

This was the assessment of Punchbowl News founder Jake Sherman, who shared the two polls Thursday morning. One was from Fox News, which explained, "If the election were today, 53 percent of voters would back the Democratic candidate in their House district and 46 percent the Republican. That 7-point edge is just outside the poll’s margin of error. Democrats were up by 5 points in April. Notably, 53 percent is the highest percentage either party has received on the generic House ballot in Fox News polling since 1996." 

As Sherman notes, that’s a “big gulf in the generic,” implying major angst toward the Republicans. But the Fox poll didn’t stop with the revelations there. As Fox elaborated, “President Donald Trump’s job approval remains near a record low, more Democrats are motivated to vote, Republicans join Democrats and independents in rating the economy negatively, and 4 in 10 voters want ‘dramatic" or "revolutionary’ change. Plus, more view the GOP as tied to elites than the Democratic Party.”

The news continues to get worse for Republicans from there. According to Fox, “Voters say Democrats can better handle income inequality (+22 points), healthcare (+21), inflation (+10), the economy (+9), and foreign policy (+7). Republicans hold the advantage on crime (+7 points), while government spending (Democrats +3) and national security (R +2) are more evenly divided. On immigration, the GOP had an 8-point edge in April. Now, Democrats are preferred by one point.” The number of those who say they face “severe” financial hardship due to inflation has shot up by 8 percent since last year, and 54 percent say they expect the economy to get worse in the coming year. What’s more, “when looking only at counties where the 2024 presidential vote was close — Trump and former Vice President Kamala Harris within 10 points — the Democratic candidate leads by 59-41 percent.”

And all that bad news is coming from a source that tends to skew in Trump and the GOP’s favor. Set alongside another new poll from Emerson, which shows a whopping 11-point lead for Democrats on a generic ballot, and it becomes clear why Sherman is saying “that’s end days for GOP.”

All of this is very bad news for Republicans heading into the midterms, where even Fox admits their chances of losing the House majority are high. It also bodes poorly for the Republican presidential candidate in 2028, as voters are making it increasingly clear that they want major change.

Thankfully, our brilliant Founding Fathers enshrined my right to keep and bear arms in our Constitution not only to deal with a tyrannical government, but to make up for that government's inability and unwillingness to protect us from the criminals they don't throw behind bars. You'll be not shocked to learn that the suspect had a lengthy rap sheet and had just been released from prison again right before he tried breaking into my house.

Anyway, even in my college days when I was more liberal, I was always strongly in favor of gun rights. The notion that just because someone does something bad with a gun means an innocent person must be punished never sat well with me. Now that I'm more conservative, my support of the Second Amendment is even stronger.

That's why when anti-gun leftists tell me they just want "common sense" gun control measures, I say, "No, thank you."

The condescension of this is my favorite part of this post by "speaker and advocate" Khary Penebaker.

Dear responsible gun owners: the laws we are asking for would not touch you. Background checks, safe storage, red flags. If your rifle is locked and your record is clean, the only thing you lose is a talking point.— Khary Penebaker (@kharyp) July 22, 2026

Kamala Harris herself laid the groundwork for this in 2007, when she told reporters, "Just because you legally possess a gun in the sanctity of your locked home doesn't mean that we're not going to walk into that home and check to see if you're being responsible."

And once we let people like Kamala Harris define what is and isn't "responsible" in my home, it's all over. I have a rather healthy collection of alcohol and wine in my kitchen. Undoubtedly, Harris and our buddy Penebaker there would consider that "irresponsible" and take my guns.

More to the point, the laws Penebaker asks for would not and have not, as far as I'm aware, done a thing to end gun violence. For street-level crime, those thugs are stealing guns or using straw purchasers—both things that skirt "common sense" gun laws.

And thanks to a loophole in LA law, he wasn't barred from owning a firearm.

That same month, former Virginia Lt. Gov. Justin Fairfax shot and killed his estranged wife, Cerina in their home before turning the gun on himself. Fairfax was a proponent of so-called "red flag" laws that would strip people of their guns if they had mental or emotional problems or accusations of violence, including domestic abuse. Fairfax had become increasingly troubled following a failed gubernatorial campaign that was derailed by allegations of sexual misconduct. When his marriage fell apart, the Washington Post reported that Fairfax became "increasingly erratic" and was ordered to leave his home and lost custody of his children. It was reported that the service of paperwork related to the divorce was the final straw that prompted the heinous act of violence. Despite all these massive red flags, the laws that Fairfax himself championed were not applied to him. Now his wife is dead, he is dead, and their teenage children are orphaned.

When it was revealed that VA Attorney General Jay Jones fantasized about putting "two bullets to the head" of Republican Todd Gilbert, and wished that Gilbert's sons would be shot and die in their mother's arms in order to get Gilbert on board with gun control legislation, the anti-gun group Giffords endorsed Jones.

It appears the gun control laws Penebaker says "won't touch" law-abiding gun owners don't touch criminals, either. Which is the point, of course. They want more gun crime, not less. His argument is the proverbial camel's nose under the tent, the start of a creep of "common sense" gun control laws that don't work, so there's more gun violence, and more incrementalism that gun control advocates hope will end with the repeal of the Second Amendment and the (attempted) confiscation of our guns. And given the plans the Democratic Socialists of America have laid out for us, it's clear we can't let that happen.
news.author: Amy Curtis

news.title: Fox News Power Rankings: The top races to watch as 36 states choose their next governor
news.text: The battle to control Congress always dominates midterm discussions, but should it? More voters will elect a governor than a U.S. senator this year. Among the key contests are what might be the GOP’s only flip opportunity, races that will show how key voter groups are responding to President Donald Trump’s second term, and even a test for Democrats in the Northwest. These elections could also decide which party holds a majority of governorships. More than half of the nation’s governors have been Republicans since 2010. But with Democrats currently holding 24 of the 50 offices, that advantage is two states away from disappearing. TOP DEMOCRAT GOVERNORS ARE HOPING TO RIDE THEIR RECORDS TO THE WHITE HOUSE. CAN THEY? Here are the top races to watch this year. Wisconsin Democrats have been going to war with each other over ideology, character and electability all across the country this year – in Wisconsin, the party is fighting all three battles in one race. At least seven notable candidates are vying for the nomination, including Democratic socialist and state Rep. Francesca Hong and former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes, who lost a U.S. Senate race here four years ago. This week, the primary got even messier. Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez ended her run after discovering she was short hundreds of thousands of dollars, and nine days after suspending his run, county executive David Crowley got back in, touting an endorsement from Gov. Tony Evers. TRUMP-BACKED FIREBRAND WINS ARIZONA GOP GOVERNOR PRIMARY, SETTING UP HIGH-STAKES SHOWDOWN Wisconsin is as competitive as it gets. It’s one of only two states that has voted for both parties for president and the Senate while also being run by governors from both parties during the Trump era (the other is Arizona). While the Democrats figure out who their candidate is, Republicans have a clear frontrunner: U.S. House Rep. Tom Tiffany, who has Trump’s backing and $3 million in the bank. It’s too early to say which party will have the edge in November. This state is a toss-up. Iowa Iowa’s Senate race is a toss-up, as Tuesday’s Power Rankings revealed, but on the governor’s side, the Democrats have opened up quite a lead. Rob Sand, the Democratic state auditor who claims he’s not a fan of either party, had the support of 53% of voters in the latest Fox News Poll, nine points ahead of his Republican opponent, Zach Lahn, a local farmer. Lahn was the surprise winner of his party’s primary after Trump endorsed a different candidate (the president has since endorsed Lahn), and appears to have consolidated his support with the GOP base. But his support for the state’s six-week abortion ban and recent relocation from Kansas could be electoral liabilities. Sand is raising more money than Lahn, and from more local donors. It’s a highly competitive race, but it starts at Lean D. Nevada In 2022, Republican Gov. Joe Lombardo’s victory over a Democratic incumbent in Nevada was proof that Hispanic voters were drifting rightward. Two years later, more confirmation, when the state voted for a Republican president for the first time in 20 years. Now there are signs that those voters are unhappy with Trump: 69% said they disapproved of the president in Fox News’ June survey. Lombardo is touting results on cutting business regulation, crime reduction and new housing projects for working-class Nevadans. He’ll face Democratic Attorney General Aaron Ford, who says voters with affordability and immigration concerns have Trump to blame. It will be a close and expensive race. It’s a toss-up in this forecast. Kansas Republicans’ best chance at flipping a state this year comes in Kansas, where Gov. Laura Kelly can’t seek a third term. Kelly is a rare species in American politics: she is one of only two Democratic governors serving in states that Trump won by double digits in 2024 (the other is Andy Beshear of Kentucky). This year, leading Democrats include state Sens. Cindy Holscher and Ethan Corson, who have made affordability the center of their messages. Seven Republicans are in the race, including state Senate President Ty Masterson, who picked up Trump’s endorsement in May. Among the big issues in the Sunflower State? Kelly’s $4 billion deal to bring the Kansas City Chiefs across state lines, which Masterson and Corson support, but some Democrats have called a "scam." It’s a competitive race, but without their incumbency advantage, the Democrats are vulnerable. This is a Lean R race. Oregon Finally, an eyebrow-raising race in the Northwest, where incumbent Democratic Gov. Tina Kotek is in a more competitive race than she should be against Republican state Sen. Christine Drazan. Oregon voted for Vice President Kamala Harris by 14 points in the last presidential election, and the state hasn’t voted for a Republican governor since 1982. Kotek is in some trouble, though, as she defends her record on housing, education and mental health services. According to local reports, the governor is far short of her goal of 36,000 new units per year, and the state has some of the worst education rankings in the country. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP That has given Drazan an opening, and not for the first time: she came within 3.5 points of defeating Kotek in 2022 (though that race included a third-party candidate). Partisanship should keep this state in Democratic hands. But not without a fight. It’s a Likely D race in this forecast.
news.summary: From Wisconsin's toss-up race to Kansas Republicans' best flip opportunity, these governor races could decide which party holds a majority of statehouses.
news.author: Rémy Numa

news.title: Jimmy Fallon Grows a Spine ... Two Years Too Late
news.text:
That's being kind. The Democrats hid President Joe Biden so aggressively he was like a lousy poll to be covered up at all costs.

Late-night satirists saw the same ol' Joe that we did. And, by and large, they said nothing.

What about Jimmy Fallon? He isn't part of the angry Left late-night alliance. Did he do his due diligence and warn the country about an addled President.

Nope. He, too mostly shied away from the topic. Meanwhile, his show spiraled downward in the ratings. While former hosts Johnny Carson and Jay Leno made "The Tonight Show" a ratings juggernaut with bipartisan yuks, Fallon delivers a milquetoast version of the far-Left rage Kimmel and co. provide on a nightly basis. 

Fallon still hits the same targets - Trump, Trump and more Trump - as his late-night peers (save Greg Gutfeld). He also joins them in ignoring Democrats behaving badly. Imagine watching a conga line of DSA candidates slam America and not mustering a single sketch about them?

The writers at Fallon's "Tonight Show" sure can. How many jokes has Fallon told about Graham Platner? 

So liberal viewers decided to go with the stiff drink, not the Shirley Temple of late-night TV. Thus, Fallon's limp ratings. Now, with Colbert gone from the scene, Kimmel has picked up some of Colbert's stranded viewers.

Fallon could have course corrected at any time, reverting to the Carson/Leno bipartisan model to see if that might spark a ratings renaissance. It wasn't a sure thing, but it was worth ye olde college try.

Now, two years after Biden left the political scene, Fallon has found the spine to mock the former president.

Courage, as Dan Rather might say!

Fallon teased Biden over the former president's upcoming book, a look back at his four years in office.

The book is about what Biden remembers from his presidency… so it’s three pages.

Get it? The president had dementia while in office, and now, but Fallon was too cowardly to speak Truth to Power. And the country suffered as a result.

Fallon even name-checked another ripe political target that everyone ignored over the last two years.

Vice President Kamala Harris.

It’s kind of a strange book because toward the end it starts being written by Kamala Harris.

It's a little late, Jimmy. The time to embrace the old-school late-night model is gone. Audiences already know what to expect from you four nights a week. That number used to be five, but NBC ordered the show to go dark on Fridays.

Gee, did the low ratings have anything to do with that move?

Either way, Fallon is finally ready to joke about Biden's sad decline. Maybe in a few years, Fallon will crack some newsy jokes about The Twitter Files, the Russian collusion lie or that "Very Fine People" hoax.

Or, he might tell another dozen jokes about President Trump's orange skin. To paraphrase "Dodgeball," It's a bold strategy, Jimmy. Let's see if it pays off for you."
news.author: Christian Toto

news.title: Hunter Biden bashes ‘a–hole’ Jake Tapper on Don Lemon’s podcast in foul-mouthed rant: ‘Full of f–king s–t’
news.text:
Hunter Biden has furiously called CNN’s Jake Tapper a “complete and utter a–hole” who is “full of f–king s–t.”

The long-troubled former first son launched his expletive-ridden tirade when asked about Tapper’s book, “Original Sin,” about Biden’s dad’s disastrous failed 2024 run for re-election.

“I think that Jake is a complete and utter a–hole,” Hunter, 56, told the CNN’s star’s former colleague, Don Lemon, on his podcast Wednesday.

The foul-mouthed former first son said he has known Tapper for 25 years, calling him a “really poor journalist.

“I don’t think he’s a very decent human being,” he said.

“I think he’s full of f–king s–t,” Hunter added, branding him “the Brick Tamland of his generation,” in reference to Steve Carell’s idiotic weatherman character in the movie “Anchorman: The Legend of Ron Burgundy.”

Tapper’s 2025 book, co-authored with Axios’ Alex Thompson, details claims of a cover-up regarding President Biden’s age and health, and features interviews with Democrat insiders who blame his decision to step down only after a poor debate performance for dooming Kamala Harris’ election campaign.

It also accuses Biden’s inner circle, including Hunter, and the First Lady Jill Biden, of shielding the president from the press, something Hunter took umbrage to.

“The way that he [Tapper] has spoken about me, the way he has spoken about my mother, the way that he has spoken about my father, the idea that I would tell you — and be full of s–t like everybody else usually is, and say, ‘Well, you know, I’d like to respect Jake’s long career in journalism and, you know, he’s done good things before’ — you know what? I don’t have to say that. I’m not running for anything,” Hunter ranted.

Jake Tapper’s representatives did not respond immediately to requests for comment, but a spokesperson for CNN told Mediate that “Jake Tapper is a respected journalist and his work speaks for itself.”

“For decades, he has challenged and scrutinized those in power, regardless of party, ideology, or title. That’s the job, and he’s done it exceptionally well. These comments are frankly ridiculous and shouldn’t warrant news coverage,” the spokesperson added.
news.summary: Hunter Biden has furiously called CNN's Jake Tapper a "complete and utter a--hole" who is "full of f--king s--t."
news.author: Anthony Blair

news.title: AOC won’t rule out 2028 presidential bid as poll shows her leading all Dems in key NH primary
news.text:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez said Wednesday she was “deeply humbled” by a new poll that shows her leading in the early 2028 presidential primary state of New Hampshire and, when pressed, would not rule out a 2028 White House bid. 

The Independent spoke with the self-described Democratic socialist congresswoman from the Bronx and Queens after a poll from the University of New Hampshire showed her leading in the first-in-the-nation primary. 

The survey showed 22 percent of would-be Democratic primary voters prefer Ocasio-Cortez, better known as AOC, while 21 percent would prefer Biden Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg. Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly comes in third at 9 percent while California Gov. Gavin Newsom placed fourth at 8 percent. 

Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), an AOC ally who won New Hampshire in 2016 and 2020, clocked in at 6 percent while former Vice President Kamala Harris garnered 5 percent. 

Ocasio-Cortez did not directly address whether she’d run, nor rule out a bid for the White House in 2028. 

“I think there's two things: One is that you know polls are polls, and a horse race is a horse race,” she said. “I am, of course, I think, very deeply humbled and grateful to you know folks who participate and convey that confidence.” 

But Ocasio-Cortez said her main focus was helping Democrats win back the majority in the House of Representatives.

“I'm honestly I think my main focus is is really genuinely just the midterms right now, and we have to make sure that we win this majority,” she said.

The one-time insurgent, who defeated former House Democratic Caucus Chairman Joe Crowley in 2018, has in many ways inherited the mantle from Sanders, the independent socialist senator who caucuses with Democrats and ran two populist campaigns. 

In 2024, AOC campaigned heavily for Harris in Nevada courting Latino voters. She also endorsed New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani during his primary challenge against disgraced former New York Gov. Andrew Cuomo. 

Since then, she’s endorsed in both deep-blue districts and swing races. In Pennsylvania’s 3rd district, she endorsed left-wing candidate Chris Rabb’s campaign in one of the bluest districts in the country. She also endorsed Randy Villegas’s successful primary in California’s 22nd district to run against incumbent Republican Rep. David Valadao.

Over the weekend, she campaigned with Abdul El-Sayed, who is running for the Democratic nomination for Michigan’s open Senate seat.

Historically, the Iowa caucus goes first in the nation before the New Hampshire primary. But in 2024, the Democratic National Committee moved South Carolina to be the first binding primary after President Joe Biden won the primary in 2020 thanks to Black voters. South Carolina and Nevada, which has a high Hispanic population, are now dueling for the right to go first. 

Harris, the most recent Democratic nominee, would likely do better in the Palmetto State. In 2020, Buttigieg narrowly beat Sanders in Iowa but fell short in southern states due to a lack of trust among Black voters.

Some Democrats expect Ocasio-Cortez to either run for president or, more likely, for Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer’s seat in 2028.

On the Republican side, Vice President JD Vance has a wide lead against Secretary of State Marco Rubio to succeed President Donald Trump as the Republican presidential nominee, with 36 percent of would-be primary voters picking Vance and 26 percent picking Rubio. 

Rubio ran for president a decade ago, but failed to put up a significant showing after a poor debate performance against then-New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie.
news.summary: Ocasio-Cortez is locked in a tight race with Pete Buttigieg in the First-in-the-Nation primary
news.author: Eric Garcia

news.title: Trump, once again, threatens TV stations. Will FCC chief follow through?
news.text:
Imagine how Republicans would react if a Democratic president reflexively threatened to yank the broadcast licenses of TV stations because he did not like their news coverage or programming decisions. President Donald Trump has been doing that since his first term, and he did it again last week after ABC and NBC declined to offer live coverage of his speech about election security.

These threats are part of an intimidation campaign that has already yielded striking concessions to Trump’s whims. His weaponization of the Federal Communications Commission is designed to chill constitutionally protected speech, establishing a precedent that Republicans may regret when their opponents take control of the White House.

Brendan Carr, the Trump-appointed FCC chairperson, seems to think broadcasters have a legal obligation to please the president. “Broadcasters must operate in the public interest,” Carr declared in March, “and they will lose their licenses if they do not.”

As Carr understands it, “the public interest” requires broadcasters to cover the president “fully, accurately, and fairly.” It may also require them to eschew war reporting that embarrasses the president and punish late-night comedians whose jokes offend him.

Any attempt to impose such a vague, open-ended standard on speakers in other media would be plainly unconstitutional. But broadcasters are different, Carr avers, because “the American people have subsidized [them] to the tune of billions of dollars by providing free access to the nation’s airwaves.”

Trump echoed that rationale for speech regulation last week, when he said broadcasters “use our public, multibillion-dollar-in-value airways for absolutely no money.” But that is plainly not true, since broadcast licenses figure in the prices that media companies pay when they acquire TV or radio stations.

Trump nevertheless relied on that false premise in arguing that the failure of ABC and NBC to give his speech the attention he thought it deserved “should mean a revocation of their licenses.” Whether or not Carr follows through on that suggestion, he has made it clear that he views correcting anti-Trump bias as an important part of his job.

That understanding of the FCC’s mission explains why Carr seriously entertained the possibility that CBS committed “broadcast news distortion” by editing a preelection interview with Kamala Harris in a way that made her seem slightly more cogent. It explains why Carr bragged about requiring changes to journalistic practices at CBS as a condition for approving Skydance Media’s acquisition of Paramount, the network’s parent company.

Carr’s conception of “the public interest” was also at the root of his most flagrant attempt to exert control over broadcast content: the regulatory threats that preceded ABC’s suspension of” Jimmy Kimmel Live” last September. It likewise underlies his warning that talk shows like “The View” can no longer rely on their longstanding exemption from the “equal time” rule — a change that would effectively prohibit them from interviewing political candidates.

So far, Carr has not delivered on Trump’s oft-repeated threats to revoke broadcast licenses. But in April, he announced an early review of ABC’s licenses, ostensibly because of concerns about “unlawful discrimination.”

Actual license revocations are not necessary to cow broadcasters, as illustrated by what happened after Trump filed a ludicrous lawsuit alleging that the Harris interview qualified as consumer fraud under Texas and federal law. Although CBS accurately noted that Trump’s complaint was “completely without merit” and vowed to “vigorously defend against it,” Paramount ultimately agreed to a $16 million settlement.

That decision followed Trump’s threats to revoke the network’s broadcast licenses and Carr’s initiation of the “news distortion” investigation. It came as the FCC was deciding whether to allow the Paramount/Skydance merger, which it finally approved a few weeks later based on the expectation of a Trump-friendlier CBS News.

That episode showed how easily “the public interest” can be conflated with the president’s personal interests. It’s a lesson that Republicans should take to heart if they allow for the possibility that they will not always control these levers of power.

Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.
news.author: Jacob Sullum

news.title: The White House Correspondents' Dinner: Decades of Mocking Conservatives
news.text:
Before the originally scheduled 2026 White House Correspondents' Dinner was postponed after a failed assassination attempt against President Trump, there was opposition from liberal circles that the usual tradition of having a comedian perform was being replaced by mentalist Oz Pearlman. With the dinner being rescheduled for Friday, NewsBusters decided to investigate why these liberal critics were upset and found a noteworthy discrepancy in the WHCD’s comedy routines when a Republican is president versus when a Democrat is president. 

Since the dinner began airing on C-SPAN in 1993, there have been nine comedy routines during a Republican presidency. NewsBusters analysts found that 65 percent of jokes targeted conservatives during these dinners. By contrast, during the 18 comedy routines during Democratic presidencies, only 49 percent of jokes targeted liberals. 

A History of White House Correspondents' Dinner Comedy Routines

Clinton Era

During his presidency, President Bill Clinton was joked about 25 times out of 228 total jokes (11 percent). Vice President Al Gore was only joked about nine times (4 percent), and six of those were in 2000. In total, conservatives were joked about 55 percent of the time. This was driven largely by Al Franken’s 1996 routine that featured 39 jokes about conservatives, 11 about liberals, and two non-partisan jokes. 

Beyond percentages and counts, there is the quality of jokes. During the Clinton era, some of the nastiness that would define later dinners would begin to surface. For example, Elayne Boosler used her 1993 performance to attack George H.W. Bush and boost abortion, “It wasn’t any better under President Bush. Bush was so interesting to me. He was against abortion, but for capital punishment. Spoken like a true fisherman. Throw ‘em back, kill em when they’re bigger.” 

Boosler also got in a gratuitous shot at Phyllis Schlafly, “Phyllis Schlafly, I have a terrible, disgusting fantasy I would like to do to this woman. Hell, I’ll tell you. I’ll never see you again. It’s awful, but I think she deserves it. You know what I would like to do to Phyllis Schlafly? I’d like to take her into a room and tie her to a chair and force her to watch people minding their own business.”

Franken used both his 1994 and 1996 appearances to feud with Rush Limbaugh. In ’94, he retorted, “Rush Limbaugh is what I call a disinfotainer. He entertains by spreading disinformation.” In ’96, he did some self-promotion, “I’d like to take a moment to mention my book, Rush Limbaugh is a Big Fat Idiot and Other Observations.”

Ahead of the 2000 election, Jay Leno had one take that, 16 years later, aged rather poorly, “Donald Trump running for president. He once said in an interview, 'People just want to be told the truth,' and he’s the type of guy, the type of straight-shooter, that people would vote for. Really? A real estate owner who’s a casino owner wants to be a politician. Isn’t that pretty much the triple crown of lying right there?”

Also on the 2000 election, Leno claimed, “Bush is trying to attract more people to the Republican Party, said he wants to create a big tent, which is also the Republican’s housing policy for the poor.” 

Commenting on that year’s GOP primary, Leno also quipped, “You know it’s sad, I didn’t enjoy seeing McCain and Bush attacking each other during the campaign. Remember the glory days of the Republican Party when they were all united against the poor and minorities? What happened there?” 

However, there was also Ray Romano in 1998, who told only three political jokes, including one about conservatives, one about liberals, and one non-partisan joke. Romano confessed to not being particularly interested in politics and was content to talk about what it is like to be the parent of twins. Romano’s apolitical routine would soon stand out as the exception to the rule.

When he was in office, President George W. Bush was the butt of 31 of 125 jokes (25 percent) from WHCD comedians. Vice President Dick Cheney received nine (7 percent). During the Bush administration 63 percent of jokes targeted conservatives.

Interestingly enough, most Bush-attended dinners were not especially nasty. Darrell Hammond (2001), Drew Carey (2002), Cedric the Entertainer (2005), and Rich Little (2007) all failed to reach double-digit jokes about conservatives. Hammond actually preferred to go back and mostly joke about Clinton and Gore, while Little just did impressions.

The exception and definite low point of the Bush era was without a doubt Stephen Colbert’s 2006 performance, where he did his entire bit in his satirical Colbert Report character and told 20 jokes about conservatives versus only two about liberals. Addressing Bush, Colbert said, “We both get it. Guys like us, we’re not some brainiacs on the nerd patrol. We’re not members of the factanista. We go straight from the gut.” 

When talking about Bush’s approval rating, Colbert added, “We know that polls are just a collection of statistics that reflect what people are thinking in 'reality' and reality has a well-known liberal bias.”

Colbert also attacked the media for not being liberal enough, “Over the past five years, you people were so good over tax cuts, WMD intelligence, the effect of global warming. We Americans didn’t want to know, and you had the courtesy not to try to find out.”

He further urged reporters to “Write that novel you got kicking around in your head. You know, the one about the intrepid Washington reporter with the courage to stand up to the administration. You know, fiction.”

Additionally, he told them, “And then you write, 'Oh, they’re just rearranging the deck chairs on the Titanic.' First of all, that is a terrible metaphor. This administration is not sinking. This administration is soaring. If anything, they are rearranging the deck chairs on the Hindenburg.”

Barack Obama's presidency is proof that numbers do not necessarily tell the whole story. Obama was on the receiving end of 50 out of 445 jokes (11 percent) while Vice President Joe Biden was joked about 22 times (5 percent). However, liberals were joked about a majority of the time, at 52 percent.

The performers joked about Obama in the “you tease the ones you love” kind of way. For instance, in 2009, Wanda Sykes told Obama, “It’s hard to poke fun at the president because he’s so likeable. Everybody likes him. T-shirts and bumper stickers. Listening to the radio and people are dedicating songs, ‘I like to send out always and forever to Mr. President.’ People love you.” 

By contrast, Sykes compared Rush Limbaugh to the 9/11 terrorists and wished death upon him, “I think maybe Rush Limbaugh was the 20th hijacker, but he was just so strung out on OxyContin he missed his flight” and, in allusion to Limbaugh’s “I hope he fails” remarks, “I hope his kidneys fail. How about that?”

In 2011, Seth Meyers had a hot take that altered history, “And then of course, there’s Donald Trump. Donald Trump has been saying that he will run for president as a Republican, which is surprising since I just assumed he was running as a joke.”

Jimmy Kimmel claimed in 2012, “I actually have my own theory about Lincoln’s death. I think John Wilkes Booth was innocent. I don’t even think it was an assassination. I believe that Abraham Lincoln had a vision about what the Republican Party would become in 150 years, and he shot himself.”

In 2014, Joel McHale quipped, “Good evening, Mr. President, or as Paul Ryan refers to you, yet another inner city minority relying on the federal government to feed and house your family,” and “E! is also home to the Kardashians, who believe it or not, are Republicans, and I know that because they’re always trying to screw black people.”

The next year, in 2015, Cecily Strong got in some abortion promotion, “I promise, since I’m only a comedian, I’m not going to try to tell you politicians how to do politics or whatever. That’s not my job. That’d be like you guys telling me what to do with my body. I mean, can you even imagine?”

She also gushed over Obama, “You’re a lot like Madonna. You’ve both given this country so much, but in a year and half, you gotta stop.”

Larry Wilmore performed at Obama’s last WHCD in 2016 and made sure to constantly play the race card, “Welcome to negro night here at Washington, or as Fox News would report, ‘two thugs disrupt elegant dinner in DC.’”

On Obama’s hair, Wilmore added, “Your hair is so white, it tried to punch me at a Trump rally.”

Wilmore also claimed, “I am not surprised Donald Trump is happening to America because I watch movies. I do. And every time there’s a black president something always comes to destroy the earth.”

Eventually, Wilmore’s Obama praise led to an uncensored N-word, “So, Mr. President. If I’m going to keep it a hundred, ‘Yo, Barry. You did it, my n*****.’”

During Donald Trump’s two terms in office, only two comedians have performed during the WHCD. In those years, 32 of 118 (28 percent) jokes were about him and six were about Vice President Mike Pence (5 percent). Since no comedian has performed in Trump’s second term, there has not been an opportunity to joke about Vice President JD Vance. From 2017-2018, Hasan Minhaj and Michelle Wolf told 66 percent of their jokes about conservatives.

Starting in 2017, Donald Trump broke with tradition and decided not to attend the dinner, which led Minhaj to claim, “The leader of our country is not here, and that’s because he lives in Moscow.”

Minhaj also claimed that Attorney General “Jeff Sessions couldn’t be here tonight. He was busy doing a pre-Civil War reenactment. On his RSVP he just wrote ‘No’… which just happens to be his second favorite N-word.”

Trump’s absence also led Minhaj to try to reframe the dinner as some First Amendment thing, “Now’s the time to be a liar, and Donald Trump is liar-in-chief, and remember you guys are public enemy number one. You are his biggest enemy. Journalists, ISIS, normal-length ties, and somehow you’re the bad guys.”

Minhaj also claimed, “But the president didn’t show up because Donald Trump doesn’t care about free speech. The man who tweets everything that enters his head doesn’t acknowledge the amendment that allows him to do it.”

The next year, Michelle Wolf continued the trend of female comedians using the WHCD to push abortion, “Mike Pence is also very anti-choice. He thinks abortion is murder, which, first of all, don’t knock it until you try it, and when you do try it, really knock it. You know, you gotta get that baby out of there.” 

She also had some colorful words about Ivanka Trump, “She was supposed to be an advocate for women, but it turns out she’s about as helpful to women as an empty box of tampons. She’s done nothing to satisfy women. So, I guess, like father, like daughter…She does clean up nice, though. Ivanka cleans up nice. She’s the diaper genie of the administration. On the outside, she looks sleek, but the inside, it’s still of shit.”

Wolf’s routine is most remembered for her attacks on White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders, “Every time Sarah steps up to the podium, I get excited because I’m not really sure what we’re going to get. You know, a press briefing, a bunch of lies, or divided into softball teams” and “Like what’s Uncle Tom but for white women who disappoint other white women?”

During his presidency, Joe Biden was the target of 19 out of 144 jokes (13 percent). Vice President Kamala Harris was not joked about at all. In total, liberals were joked about 49 percent of the time during the Biden administration.

After a two-year absence due to COVID, Trevor Noah highlighted the WHCD’s return by claiming, “I’m sure wherever Roger Ailes is now, he’s looking up and smiling.”

The next year, Roy Wood Jr. invited attendees to vote Democrat, “Speaking of drag queens, can we stop with the grooming stuff. Can you stop talking about that? Drag queens are not at a school to groom your kids. Stop it. And even if they were, most of them kids going to get shot at school. It ain’t no problem. Don’t groan. Pass legislation.”

Wood also accused conservatives of being racist, “Anti-CRT policies are an attack on black history and an attempt to erase the contributions of black people from the history books…. You are trying to erase black people and a lot of black people wouldn’t mind that erasure as long as that black person is Clarence Thomas.”

In 2024, two months before the infamous Trump-Biden debate, Colin Jost praised Biden, “But before I begin tonight, can we just acknowledge how refreshing it is to see a president of the United States at an event that doesn’t begin with a bailiff saying ‘all rise'?" 

Looking ahead to Trump’s VP search, Jost also quipped, “Now that OJ’s dead, who is the new frontrunner for Trump’s VP? Is it Diddy? And by the way, if Trump did select Diddy as his running mate, I bet this race would still be tied.”

Jost also made sure to get in his Biden endorsement, “He [Jost's grandfather] voted for you, and the reason he voted for you is because you’re a decent man. My grandpa voted for decency, and decency is why we are all here today.”

Since 1993, WHCD comedians have told 51 percent of their jokes about conservatives. That comes out to 533 out of 1,055. However, that percentage does not tell the whole story. While the ratio of jokes may be close to 1:1, the ratio of GOP dinners with comedians to Democratic dinners with comedians is 1:2. It also does not take into account the relative nastiness of the jokes. Jokes about how nobody reads USA Today or watches CNN are not nearly as nasty as wishing death upon Rush Limbaugh. Jokes about Obama’s hair color are not as nasty as calling conservatives Uncle Toms, and people who insist that the WHCD needs a comedian to “speak truth to power” should not be allowed to pretend otherwise.

Methodology: A target for a joke could be defined as a politician, media personality, or media organization. In this study, Fox News, Newsmax, and their on-air talent are considered conservatives unless there an obvious reason to do otherwise. Other media organizations and personalities are considered to be part of the liberal media. The exception would be C-SPAN, which is considered non-partisan. Jokes about the media or journalists as a monolith were not included.
news.summary:
Before the originally scheduled 2026 White House Correspondents' Dinner was postponed after a failed assassination attempt against President Trump, there was opposition from liberal circles that the usual tradition of having a comedian perform was being replaced by mentalist Oz Pearlman. With the dinner being rescheduled for Friday, NewsBusters decided to investigate why these liberal critics were upset and found a noteworthy discrepancy in the WHCD’s comedy routines when a Republican is president versus when a Democrat is president. 
  
      
                 



      
            Media Research Center
      
  
Since the dinner began airing on C-SPAN in 1993, there have been nine comedy routines during a Republican presidency. NewsBusters analysts found that 65 percent of jokes targeted conservatives during these dinners. By contrast, during the 18 comedy routines during Democratic presidencies, only 49 percent of jokes targeted liberals. A History of White House Correspondents' Dinner Comedy RoutinesClin

news.title: David M. Drucker: The US could ‘abolish ICE.' Then what?
news.text:
It would be political folly for the Democratic Party to fully embrace calls to "abolish ICE," at least without adopting a substitute blueprint for strong immigration enforcement.

That's not to say Immigration and Customs Enforcement hasn't abused its power during President Donald Trump's second term. American citizens have been harassed, detained, even killed. Immigrants with legal status or those that lived in the U.S. for years have suffered similarly, as happened yet again this month with the killings of Johan Sebastian Guerrero in Maine and Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Texas. Even the conditions and safety of detention have come under scrutiny after several in-custody deaths.

Not surprisingly, many voters have soured on ICE. Half of U.S. adults said they were at least "somewhat" supportive of "abolishing ICE," including 77% of Democrats and 52% of independents, according to a YouGov survey published in March.

That explains why several Democratic congressional candidates are campaigning to "abolish ICE," in upcoming midterm elections. Among them is Michigan Senate contender Abdul El-Sayed, who like many progressive Democrats pushing to dismantle ICE, has not offered an alternative agency or strategy for handling immigration enforcement. Ditto Illinois Governor JB Pritzker, who is running for reelection and is a rumored 2028 contender. That's a mistake.

Abolishing ICE is a simplistic message that makes Democratic candidates easy pickings for Republicans. Voters' opposition to bad immigration enforcement doesn't mean they want lax enforcement. Failing to provide a detailed proposal for ICE's legitimate functions after the agency were to shut down only compounds that political miscalculation. It stokes lingering voter suspicions that Democrats don't have a problem with ICE but with the enforcement of U.S. immigration law more broadly.

That's not just my analysis. Some veteran Democratic insiders worry the party is exceedingly vulnerable on this front: Republican campaigns are sure to air advertising this fall that accuse Democrats of opposing the deportation of immigrants in the U.S. illegally who have committed violent crimes. That's a policy that remains popular, even as voters' opinions of ICE, and Trump's handling of immigration, have slumped.

"It's really important for candidates in places like Florida, Texas, Iowa, Ohio, Alaska to have a posture on crime that neutralizes the attacks that will come in the fall," veteran Democratic operative Neera Tanden told the New York Times' Thomas B. Edsall. She's not wrong. The Democrats' path to the Senate majority, if one exists, runs mostly through red states with voting majorities that prioritize border security.

Proponents of abolishing ICE usually argue that they're not opposed to immigration enforcement, that criminals in the U.S. illegally don't deserve to stay, that ICE is irreparably damaged and reforming the agency is impossible and that the abuses can only be stopped by starting from scratch with a new approach. They often emphasize that ICE is only 23 years old, a product of the government reorganization spearheaded by President George W. Bush in the aftermath of the 9/11 terrorist attacks. Immigration reform occurred pre-ICE and can continue post-ICE.

"ICE, as we know it, is an infant government agency. And I have no problem whatsoever, personally, abolishing it," Ethan Fauré, a senior research analyst at the progressive Political Research Associates told me. "The reality is that when you talk about changing ICE in some way to only find the bad people [immigrants] - however that's conceived - the reality is that… the police departments and sheriffs throughout the country are ostensibly doing that already, so ICE itself is a redundant agency."

I'm not questioning the sincerity of Democrats who are troubled by ICE and want the agency abolished. Nor am I suggesting politicians aren't justified in being aghast at the agency's misconduct. What I am questioning is the feasibility of their position. Simply put, calls to "abolish ICE" versus "reform ICE" aren't politically viable- especially if presented without a comprehensive alternative. And Democrats should know better.

President Joe Biden's response to Trump's first-term immigration tactics, which never rose to the level of his second administration, was initially to reduce enforcement - at the border and within the country. The perception of chaos, crime and unfairness sent voters scurrying back to the current president. (Yes, inflation and the economy played a major role in Trump's reelection but so did immigration policy.) It's why Biden attempted to reverse course late in his presidency and cracked down on illegal border crossings and the gaming of the asylum system. It's why then-Vice President Kamala Harris tried to recast herself as an immigration hawk when she ran for president.

Ultimately, voters' dissatisfaction with Trump, the Iran war and persistent inflation may reward Democrats with control of Congress regardless of how many of them hop on the "abolish ICE" bandwagon. But if the party wants to be in the strongest position to win the White House in 2028, it needs to reconsider, or at least develop a plan, for what happens next.

This column reflects the personal views of the author and does not necessarily reflect the opinion of the editorial board or Bloomberg LP and its owners.

David M. Drucker is a columnist covering politics and policy. He is also a senior writer for The Dispatch and the author of "In Trump's Shadow: The Battle for 2024 and the Future of the GOP."
